## Details on transfer payment programs

### Transfer payment programs of $5 million or more

#### 9-8-8 Suicide Crisis Helpline (previously known as the Pan-Canadian Suicide Prevention Service)
Type of transfer payment: Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018–19 and 2023–24
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
This project began when funding was announced in Budget 2019, whereby $21 million over five years was provided to the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health (CAMH) and its partners to implement and sustain a pan-Canadian suicide prevention service, branded as Talk Suicide Canada in June 2022. When demand for service surged during and following the COVID-19 pandemic, PHAC provided additional funds to CAMH, including through the Distress Line Investment announced in the 2020 Fall Economic Statement, to sustain and grow the capacity of Talk Suicide Canada during and beyond the COVID-19 pandemic.
The 9-8-8 Suicide Crisis Helpline is an evolution of Talk Suicide Canada and the renewed purpose of this program is to support the implementation and operation of the 9-8-8 Suicide Crisis Helpline, a three-digit number providing access to suicide prevention and emotional distress support via voice and text. Funding for 9-8-8 was announced in Budget 2023 and will end in March 2026. PHAC will undertake a review to establish a long-term funding mechanism for 9-8-8 post March 2026.
The objective of the 9-8-8 Suicide Crisis Helpline is to provide people across Canada with an easy to remember number to access live bilingual crisis support 24 hours a day, seven days a week, from trained responders using the technology of their choice, voice or text.
CAMH leads the coordination of service delivery for 9-8-8, building on its experience as lead organization for Talk Suicide Canada. Calls and texts to 9-8-8 are answered by trained responders at one of 39 local, provincial / territorial, and national crisis lines who co-deliver the service. Where possible, callers and texters are routed to the nearest available responder. A hub of trained responders provide additional capacity when a local responder is not available. All 9-8-8 network partners provide existing crisis services within their communities, and also answer 9-8-8 calls and texts.
The 9-8-8 Suicide Crisis Helpline successfully launched on November 30, 2023. Between launch and March 31, 2024, 9-8-8 received 91,828 calls (an average of 747 calls per day), and 50,811 texts (an average of 413 texts per day). Data regarding 9-8-8 is publicly available on the 9-8-8 website .
Responders for Talk Suicide Canada continued to be trained and equipped with resources, knowledge and skills to appropriately respond to people reaching out prior to the implementation of 9-8-8. In preparation for the launch of 9-8-8, training was developed along two streams: service delivery for all 9-8-8 network partner personnel, and technology training for 9-8-8 network partner personnel using the CAMH technology solution. Components included the 9-8-8 Suicide Crisis Helpline intervention manual, service delivery webinars, videos for technology foundations, technology supervisory webinars, technology drop-in sessions, etc.
Performance Indicator:
100% of responders (both volunteer and paid) have received the minimum required training specific to the Pan-Canadian Suicide Prevention Service and the 9-8-8 Suicide Crisis Helpline.
In order to engage with the sector, CAMH connected with all potential 9-8-8 network providers through different modalities to provide updates, create space for discussion and provide feedback and advice as the 9-8-8 project unfolded. Following an Expression of Interest process to become service delivery partners of 9-8-8, CAMH entered into services agreements with 39 service providers throughout Canada. Some partners provide national services for specific populations and communities, such as First Nations, Inuit and Métis, and callers under 18 years of age. In addition to an advisory committee, implementation table, and Lived Experience Caucus, working groups and committees were put in place to address service delivery, technology, marketing and communications, and equity. Meetings with provinces and territories in preparation for the implementation of 9-8-8 also took place.
Access to the service:
People living anywhere in Canada can access 9-8-8 by call or text in English and French, 24 hours a day, seven days a week.
Demand for Talk Suicide Canada increased steadily prior to the launch of 9-8-8 and as a result of the increased funding, the number of answered interactions for the suicide prevention service more than doubled in 2023-2024 compared to the previous year.
In 2023–24, 135,081 calls were answered, and 77,447 text messages, for a total of 212,528 interactions.

#### Aboriginal Head Start in Urban and Northern Communities
Type of transfer payment: Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018–19
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
To support Indigenous organizations to provide culturally appropriate early childhood development programs for First Nations, Inuit and Métis children and their families who live off reserve in urban and northern communities.
- 3,001 Indigenous children and their families participated in activities at Aboriginal Head Start in Urban and Northern Communities (AHSUNC) sites;
- 81% of AHSUNC sites have partner organizations. Organizations from various sectors collaborate with AHSUNC sites to support the needs of AHSUNC participants; and
- 100% of children enrolled in AHSUNC experienced developmental benefits in a context that celebrates Indigenous cultures and language.
Findings of audits completed in 2023–24:
Completed: No internal audits were completed in 2023–24.
Planned: No audits were planned.
Findings of evaluations completed in 2023–24:
Completed: No evaluations were completed in 2023–24.
Planned: The next evaluation is planned for 2027–28.
Engagement of applicants and recipients in 2023–24:
Recipients are engaged through targeted solicitations for ongoing program funding. Funded recipients are expected to deliver high-quality, culturally appropriate, community-based early learning programming for Indigenous children and families focused on six program components: Indigenous culture and language, education and school readiness, health promotion, nutrition, social support and parental involvement. PHAC officials continue to provide support and guidance to recipients through the implementation, monitoring and evaluation of their projects with the shared goal of advancing the broader objectives of the Indigenous Early Learning and Child Care (IELCC) Framework.
In implementing this framework through horizontal collaboration, PHAC's AHSUNC-IELCC Partnership Strategy guides engagement with applicants and recipient partners. This active and ongoing outreach supports partners' involvement in existing IELCC processes to contribute to holistic, Indigenous-led decision outcomes, including the development of allocation methodologies, results-based frameworks, and future priority setting.

#### Addressing Posttraumatic Stress Disorder and Trauma in Those Most Affected By COVID
Type of transfer payment: Grants and Contributions
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018–19
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
Through Budget 2021, the Government of Canada provided $50 million over two years starting in 2021–22 to support projects to deliver, evaluate and build evidence regarding effective interventions to address posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and trauma in health care workers, frontline and essential workers and others affected by the COVID-19 pandemic. This investment also supports scaling-up effective interventions for broader reach and/or adapting and piloting them with new groups of people or different means of delivery, such as virtual modalities.
- Promoting mental health and preventing mental illness in those affected by or at risk of PTSD and trauma related to the COVID-19 pandemic;
- Building evidence about effective interventions to address trauma and PTSD in the pandemic and recovery context; and
- Enhancing capacity of individuals, service providers and organizations to address trauma and PTSD.
In 2023–24, PHAC supported 14 projects as recipients implemented and tested promising approaches and adaptations for those at risk of experiencing PTSD and trauma, including initiatives that helped reduce stigma, enhanced trauma-informed practice, removed barriers to care and improved help-seeking and system navigation. Examples of initiatives funded through this investment include:
- a resilience-building intervention for public safety personnel;
- a peer support app for health care workers; and
- and a trauma-informed training initiative for harm reduction workers.
As of March 31, 2024, preliminary findings indicate that these funded projects have trained and reached over 111,536 service providers, including nurses, physicians, public safety personnel, teachers, long-term care workers, paramedics, firefighters, Veterans, and others at over 160 sites across the country. As a result of this funding, 88% of project participants reported increased knowledge and/or skills on preventing PTSD and trauma and 77% of project participants reported positive change in behaviour.

#### Canada Prenatal Nutrition Program
Type of transfer payment: Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2017–18
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Canada Prenatal Nutrition Program (CPNP) is intended to improve the health of pregnant women and people and their infants who face challenges that may put their health at risk. Funded community-level projects are aimed at increasing healthy birth weights, promoting and supporting healthy nutrition during pregnancy and postpartum, promoting and supporting breastfeeding, and supporting positive health behaviours and well-being. This program also promotes the creation of partnerships within communities and strengthens community capacity to increase support for pregnant women and people and new parents and guardians who face challenges that may put their health at risk.
In 2022–23, which is the last available results since they are collected every two years, the CPNP provided programming to approximately 41,000 participants, including pregnant and postpartum women and people, and other parents and guardians
The CPNP was shown to leverage partnerships and additional funding sources, as demonstrated through the following results:
- 63% of projects worked with more than three different types of partners in 2022–23 CPNP projects partnered most frequently with health organizations, food/clothing banks or similar organizations and community organizations.
- 41% of projects were able to leverage funds from other sources such as provincial, territorial, regional, or municipal governments in 2022–23.

#### Community Action Program for Children
Type of transfer payment: Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2017–18
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Community Action Program for Children (CAPC) aims to promote the health and development of children aged 0 to 6 years, who face challenges that put their health at risk. Funded community-level projects aim to develop and deliver a range of comprehensive and culturally appropriate early intervention and prevention activities aimed at improving health behaviours and overall health and wellbeing. This program also promotes the creation of partnerships within communities and strengthening community capacity to increase support for children and their families who face challenges that may put their health at risk.
In 2022–23, which is the last available results since they are collected every two years, CAPC provided services to over 191,000 participants.
The CAPC has been successful in leveraging partnerships and additional funding sources. For example:
- 71% of CAPC projects worked with more than three different types of partners in 2022–23. CAPC projects partnered most frequently with health organizations, community organizations, and library/literacy organizations.
- 56% of projects were able to leverage funds from other sources such as provincial, territorial, regional, or municipal governments in 2022–23.
Data collected in 2018 showed that a significant proportion of CAPC participants experience challenges that may affect their health and wellbeing. CAPC has also been successful in helping to mitigate health inequalities for the program participants. For example, the CAPC program contributed to building the knowledge and skills of parents and guardians, which supports maternal, child and family health. A 2018 survey of participants revealed that, as a result of participating in CAPC:
- 85% reported that their parenting skills had improved;
- 85% reported knowing more about how to keep their child healthy; and
- 80% reported that their child is better able to express themself.
Additional evidence showed that 90% of respondents reported that their child's health and wellbeing improved, 86% of respondents reported having a better relationship with their child and 89% reported having more people to talk to when they need support as a result of coming to the CAPC program.

#### Contribution Funding for the Centre for Aging and Brain Health Innovation
Type of transfer payment: Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2022–23
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
This funding program will help innovators accelerate the development, dissemination, scalability and promotion of promising aging and brain health innovations. The Centre for Aging and Brain Health Innovation (CABHI) supports innovators through an integrated suite of programs to help accelerate the innovation-to-commercialization process. CABHI does this by providing financial support for innovation projects, facilitating co-development with end-users (e.g., patients, caregivers and healthcare providers) and offering tailored services for innovators (e.g., scientific advice, training in innovation and health systems and developing procurement options and marketing plans).
- Increased and accelerated development, validation and scaling of solutions that enable older adults and people living with brain health issues, including dementia, to live in the setting of their choice and remain active in their communities;
- Improved products, programs and services that support dementia prevention and enhance the quality of life of people living with dementia and caregivers;
- Expanded delivery of high-quality, accessible virtual care for physical, mental and cognitive health services and supports; and
- Enhanced knowledge mobilization related to aging and brain health.
Performance indicators:
- 77 projects approved and launched in 2023–24;
- 68 unique solutions funded within CABHI's portfolio of innovation in 2023–24;
- 20,668 individuals engaged in testing and validation of CABHI's new products, practices or services;
- 128 unique members within CABHI's Lived Experience Advisory Panel in 2023–24;
- 10 solutions introduced into practice, brought to market or adopted by care provider organizations in 2023–24;
- 24 solutions adopted or procured beyond CABHI-funded projects in 2023–24;
- 126 jobs created – not including CABHI personnel in 2023–24; and
- $45,697,778 of secured investments (public and private) by CABHI-funded innovators or companies.

#### COVID-19 Proof of Vaccination Fund for Provinces and Territories
Type of transfer payment: Statutory
Type of appropriation: Not applicable
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: Not applicable
Link to departmental result:
- Result 2.1: Infectious diseases are prevented and controlled
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
PHAC developed a Canadian COVID-19 Proof of Vaccination Credential (PVC) specification that is issued by provinces and territories. This initiative required extensive federal, provincial, territorial and Indigenous engagement.
Building on this work, PHAC also established and administered the federal $300 million COVID-19 Proof of Vaccination Fund to support provinces and territories for costs incurred to implement the PVC programs. An exchange of ministerial letters is required to access the Fund.
An additional six provinces and territories: Saskatchewan, Manitoba, New Brunswick, Newfoundland and Labrador, Prince Edward Island, and Nunavut have accessed the Fund. This represents nearly $78.1 million in transfers.
To date, 12 provinces and territories have accessed the Fund, representing $224.1 million in transfers.

Once reviewed, provincial and territorial allotments have been released and the exchange of ministerial letters has been posted on the COVID-19 Proof of Vaccination Fund webpage .

#### Dementia Strategic Fund and Enhanced Dementia Surveillance Program
Type of transfer payment: Grants and Contributions
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018
Link to departmental results:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
- Result 1.2: Canadians have improved health behaviours
- Result 1.3: Chronic diseases are prevented
Link to the department's program inventory:
- Evidence for Health Promotion, and Chronic Disease and Injury Prevention
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
These transfer payment programs support the implementation of Canada's National Dementia Strategy .
As of 2020–21, roughly 477,000 people in Canada aged 65 years and older were living with diagnosed dementia. Of those, nearly two-thirds are women. As Canada's population ages, it is expected that the total number of people in Canada living with dementia will continue to rise despite the decreasing trend in the rate of new cases.
These programs supported the vision of a Canada in which all people living with dementia and their caregivers are valued and supported, that their quality of life is optimized, and that dementia is well understood, treated effectively and prevented for future generations.
The Dementia Strategic Fund supported progress on the strategy's national objectives through the development and implementation of the following initiatives: a national public education campaign focused on reducing stigma and enabling communities to be more dementia-inclusive, and reducing dementia risk. The projects focused on:
- Awareness-raising to prevent dementia, reduce stigma, and support communities in becoming more dementia-inclusive;
- Improving access to high-quality dementia guidance;
- Enhancing provincial and territorial online dementia information resources;
- Supporting the World Health Organization to accelerate the implementation of the global action plan on the public health response to dementia 2017–25 by building national capacity to provide comprehensive responses to the Global Dementia Observatory, and to strengthen global efforts on risk reduction; and
- Public opinion research to deepen the understanding of dementia in Canada.
The Enhanced Dementia Surveillance Initiative supports the enhancement and expansion of data to inform dementia-related public health actions.
PHAC completed three solicitations under the Dementia Strategic Fund (DSF) in 2023–24. One solicitation resulted in two projects focused on enhancing evidence-based online dementia resources for people in Canada on provincial and territorial governmental websites, including updating and/or expanding online dementia-related content and making dementia-related content more accessible. The remaining two solicitations resulted in two projects with the World Health Organization (WHO) to update the 2019 WHO guidelines on dementia risk reduction, and to assist WHO Member States to participate in the Global Dementia Observatory, which enables better tracking of global progress and supports implementation of the WHO global action plan on dementia.
A total of 40 projects were funded through the DSF with a focus on reaching populations and individuals identified as likely to be at higher risk for dementia and/or face barriers to equitable care. Across the 40 DSF projects, several of these key populations were reached: Indigenous communities (20% of projects), 2SLGBTQI+ (10%), racialized communities (30%), Official Language Minority Communities (20%); and persons living with intellectual and developmental disabilities (5%).
PHAC's 2023–24 national public dementia education campaign focused on confronting stigma and reducing the risk of developing dementia. The campaign included a variety of efforts to reach people in Canada such as a digital advertising campaign, articles for use by media organizations, and a risk reduction digital poster for Indigenous audiences across Canada. Traffic to Canada.ca dementia web pages was seven times higher during the campaign period compared to the five-month period prior to the campaign. Approximately 480,000 visits occurred between September 18, 2023, and March 31, 2024. Two video testimonials from people in Canada living with dementia were featured in the stigma-focused portion of the campaign and were shown 1.1. million times. During the risk reduction phase of the campaign, digital ads were shown 62.2 million times and users clicked on the ads more than 400,000 times.
In 2023–24, two public opinion research projects were completed and two new public opinion research projects were launched to strengthen PHAC's understanding of dementia in Canada. The projects focused on stigma, dementia-inclusive communities, preparedness and response needs of people living with dementia and caregivers during climate-related emergencies. Key questions from previous surveys were also included to examine changes over time since the implementation of the national dementia strategy. For example, the data gathered suggests that the number of people intentionally taking steps to reduce their risk of developing dementia has increased from 22% in 2020 to 34% in 2023 and more people agree that there are things that can be done to reduce the risk of dementia (81% in 2023 compared to 74% in 2020).
The Enhanced Dementia Surveillance Program addressed data gaps on people living with dementia and their caregivers through the completion of nine projects. Overall, these projects aimed to address important data gaps concerning the cause, progression stages, impacts, sociodemographic characteristics, risks and protective factors of dementia as well as dementia caregivers. Throughout the Program, 94% of identified relevant organizations and groups contributed data to support dementia surveillance. Further, there were 11 evidence products released in fiscal year 2023–24, linked to dementia surveillance. Some examples include:
- A public dementia dashboard that provides demographic and clinical information on dementia in primary care settings across Canada;
- The Comprehensive Approach to Dementia Data Monitoring in Canada (CADDM) aims to provide a more comprehensive set of important factors linked to dementia;
- The review "Potentially Modifiable Risk Factors for Dementia and Mild Cognitive Impairment" which aimed to explore potentially modifiable risk factors for mild and major neurocognitive disorders, including dementia;
- The study "Examining the immediate and ongoing impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on population-based estimates of dementia" which aimed to examine the immediate and ongoing impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on identifying dementia with health administrative data; and
- The study "Prevalence of dementia among people experiencing homelessness in Ontario, Canada" which aimed to explore the prevalence of dementia in people experiencing homelessness in Ontario.
Published and future findings that result from this initiative that will help inform public health actions can be found on the Enhanced Dementia Surveillance Initiative website .

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Healthy Canadians and Communities Fund (HCCF) focuses on promoting healthy living among people living in Canada who face health inequalities and are at greater risk of developing chronic diseases including diabetes, cardiovascular disease and cancer. The HCCF supports interventions that address behavioural risk factors for chronic diseases, including physical inactivity, unhealthy eating and tobacco use. The program also aims to create physical and social environments that are known to support better health among people living in Canada.
HCCF priorities include investing in interventions that address health inequalities among priority populations, encouraging the participation of multiple sectors in chronic disease prevention and uncovering new ways to address risk factors for chronic disease. The HCCF will also continue to explore new approaches to support people in Canada in leading healthier lives.
In 2023–24, the HCCF provided funding to 72 projects. This included funding for new interventions that started in that year, interventions which began in previous years and continued to be implemented in 2023–24, and those that ended in 2023–24. Outcomes for continuing and completed projects during 2023–24 included:
- More than 550,000 individuals participated in various interventions;
- 61% of participants increased their knowledge of chronic diseases or risk/protective factors;
- 48% of participants demonstrated improved skills or abilities to support healthy behavior;
- 41% of participants reported that their social environments improved to support ongoing healthy behavior and/or well-being;
- 67% of the built environment-dedicated projects demonstrated improvement in the environment to support healthy behavior, based on baseline data from 2023;
- 53% of participants improved their health behaviors; and
- 39% of participants improved their health status.
Additional elements which contributed to the success of projects included:
- Partnerships that provided financial and in-kind resources, such as staff, equipment, community spaces and evaluation expertise that helped build meaningful connections in the community. For example, a project led by the Canadian Red Cross aimed to engage older adults facing inactivity, food insecurity, and social isolation. The project co-designed a common framework for social prescribing in Canada by leveraging expertise from intersectoral partners from the health field, community, academia, and people with lived experiences; and
- Meaningful engagement with priority populations helped build trust, fostered cultural competency, and promoted ongoing participation in all aspects of the interventions.

#### HIV and Hepatitis C Community Action Fund
Type of transfer payment: Grants and Contributions
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018–19
Link to departmental result:
- Result 2.1: Infectious diseases are prevented and controlled
Link to the department's program inventory:
- Communicable Disease and Infection Control
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this program is to reduce the incidence and health impacts of sexually transmitted and blood-borne infections (STBBI) in Canada.
Aligned with the Pan-Canadian Framework for Action on STBBI and the Government of Canada Five Year Action Plan on STBBI (2024-2030), the goals of the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) and Hepatitis C Community Action Fund (CAF) include:
- Community-based efforts to reach key populations, including those unaware of their Hepatitis C status and link them to testing, prevention, treatment and care;
- Community designs and implementations of evidence-based frontline projects to prevent new and reoccurring infections;
- High-impact interventions brought to scale so that more people benefit from them; and
- Community-based efforts to reduce stigma toward populations disproportionately affected by STBBI, including people living with HIV or hepatitis C.
Community-based organizations play a critical role in delivering sexual health education and prevention activities, developing culturally adapted resources, and conducting community capacity-building activities due to their expertise in identifying and implementing culturally and contextually appropriate solutions. Through the CAF, PHAC funds projects and initiatives that focus on the following key populations:
- Gay, bisexual men and other men who have sex with men;
- People who use drugs;
- First Nations, Inuit and Métis Peoples;
- Racialized people and migrants, particularly from regions with high HIV or hepatitis C prevalence;
- Sex workers and their clients;
- People living in or recently released from correctional facilities;
- Transgender and non-binary people;
- People living with HIV or hepatitis C;
- Women among these groups, as appropriate; and
- Youth among these groups, as appropriate.
In 2023–24, PHAC provided $26.4 million through the HIV and Hepatitis C CAF to over 130 community-based projects across Canada to address HIV, hepatitis C and other sexually transmitted and blood-borne infections. While projects are still currently reporting on their impact from 2023–24, the complete data suggests that just under 43,000 individuals from the key populations and just over 16,000 individuals from target audiences were reached in 2022–23 across the country.
To expedite the translation of STBBI-related knowledge, the National STBBI Knowledge Broker function of the CAF, carried out by the Canadian AIDS Treatment Information Exchange (CATIE), distributed 1.2 million educational tools and resources to providers, community organizations, public health and healthcare providers and policy decision-makers. A total of 44 HIV and hepatitis C courses were developed and delivered to more than 4,000 learners and nine webinars were delivered to more than 3,500 attendees. In response to continued syphilis outbreaks, a special webinar series was developed in November 2023, which attracted over 1,000 individuals. This successfully contributed to CAF's outcomes with approximately 95% of participants reporting that they benefited from increased education and knowledge as a result of CATIE's knowledge mobilization activities. Participants increased their knowledge of effective evidence-based HIV, hepatitis C or related STBBI prevention measures, with 67% reporting a policy or practice change that they had implemented to improve the cultural safety and stigma-free nature of STBBI testing, prevention, treatment and ongoing care and support. Frontline service providers and community organizations, public health and healthcare professionals as well as policy decision makers and leaders participated in knowledge exchange activities.
In August 2022, the HIV Self-Test (HIVST) Initiative was announced, with a time-limited investment of $8 million for the 2022–23 fiscal year. Its goal was to provide proof that low barrier access to HIV self-testing could reach key populations who had not been previously tested for HIV. In 2023–24, an additional $8.6 million in funding was secured to continue the community-based initiative until the end of March 2024. This funding supported the procurement and distribution of over 140,000 HIV self-test kits to more than 54,000 individuals in 425 participating community-based organizations across Canada.
The initiative supported the development of print and digital resources to promote HIV testing, demonstrate how to utilise the self-test and provide resources and activities that offered linkages to prevention, care, treatment and support programs. For one national program, the percentage of first-time testers reached was 46%, while a second national program reported reaching an average of 57% first-time testers and people who had not been tested within the previous year. Low-barrier access to HIV self-testing has now been demonstrated to be an effective means of reaching undiagnosed persons. As provinces, territories and other federal departments play a key role in HIV testing programs and operations, PHAC will be working to extend awareness of these results to encourage opportunities to integrate self-testing into existing care pathways.
An additional $2.2 million was invested in time-limited community-based funding that supported targeted community-based responses to promote Undetectable = Untransmittable (U=U) and address infectious and congenital syphilis. The activities funded for U=U included the development of an online U=U Continuing Medical Education (CME) course for healthcare providers, a provincial awareness U=U campaign in Québec that appeared in 18 regional newspapers, magazines, professional billboards, and social media posts, and a national consultation with people living with HIV that developed a U=U training program.

#### Immunization Partnership Fund
Type of transfer payment: Grants and Contributions
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2022–23

- Result 2.1: Infectious diseases are prevented and controlled
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Immunization Partnership Fund (IPF) is a key component of PHAC's efforts to support confidence and increase uptake of vaccines to control and prevent the spread of vaccine-preventable diseases. The IPF is an important lever to identify and help address gaps in vaccine acceptance. It aims to empower people in Canada to make informed vaccine decisions, particularly those in underserved communities and with persisting gaps in vaccine coverage. The program focuses on three areas: building capacity for healthcare providers, supporting community-based vaccination education, promotion and outreach, as well as capacity building for evidence-based vaccination communication. The IPF supports equity-promoting initiatives that provide culturally safe and evidence-informed education, promotion initiatives, and activities that reduce barriers to vaccination.
In 2023–24, the IPF provided $10 million to develop and deliver 55 community-based organizations' promotional and educational activities. These activities were designed to increase confidence in respiratory illness vaccines, such as influenza and COVID-19. They also promoted catching-up of routine vaccinations (e.g., routine childhood vaccinations, HPV, tetanus, shingles, etc.) for individuals who may have missed or delayed these vaccinations due to the pandemic. In collaboration with their network of partners, IPF funding recipients developed more than 400 unique evidence-informed, equity-promoting and culturally safe vaccination resources, activities and services for people across Canada. Through the use of barrier-reducing approaches, IPF projects engaged with more than 54,100 individuals through in-person programming efforts. It was reported that 20 IPF projects facilitated more than 2,600 vaccination opportunities which resulted in over 17,500 individuals receiving a vaccine.

In 2023–24, IPF convened communities of practice to provide opportunities for partnership-building and knowledge exchange amongst recipients. This allowed recipients to share experiences regarding the transition from COVID-19 projects to the promotion of routine and seasonal vaccination across the life course. Additionally, the engagement allowed for the sharing of innovative practices and lessons learned from their initiatives promoting both COVID-19 and routine vaccines for diverse age groups and communities across Canada.

#### Immunization Surveillance
Type of transfer payment: Grants and Contributions
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates

- Result 2.1: Infectious diseases are prevented and controlled
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this program is to protect the health of people in Canada and support public health action by enhancing the evidence base on vaccine coverage, safety and effectiveness. This informs the implementation of programs and targeting of interventions while complementing existing surveillance systems, using new and diverse data sources and filling evidence gaps identified through regular surveillance activities.
The program aims to:
- Bolster Canadian scientific capacity and preparedness by supporting researchers in the testing and studying of new or emerging adverse events following immunization, identified through vaccine safety monitoring and reporting activities;
- Support provincial and territorial partners to enhance their data collection, reporting and analysis related to vaccine safety and coverage; and
- Establish and leverage new evidence sources to complement existing surveillance systems and fill gaps by: Supporting the development and testing of new approaches, methodologies and tools; Building capacity in organizations across Canada to explore innovative surveillance approaches to advance understanding of how vaccine coverage and perception of effectiveness may differ across specific populations (e.g., Indigenous, 2SLGBTQIA+, low socio-economic status, English/French minority languages communities, racialized communities); and Generating surveillance information, establishing diverse data sources and other surveillance-related evidence not easily captured by governments.
- Supporting the development and testing of new approaches, methodologies and tools;
- Building capacity in organizations across Canada to explore innovative surveillance approaches to advance understanding of how vaccine coverage and perception of effectiveness may differ across specific populations (e.g., Indigenous, 2SLGBTQIA+, low socio-economic status, English/French minority languages communities, racialized communities); and
- Generating surveillance information, establishing diverse data sources and other surveillance-related evidence not easily captured by governments.
Funding for 2023–24 allowed continued support for two projects related to long-term outcomes of post-vaccination adverse events. The first is the McMaster University project, which started in 2021. It leverages the world renowned Platelet Immunology Laboratory, and contributes to the vaccine-induced immune thrombotic thrombocytopenia (VITT) knowledge base through the publication of over 20 papers and delivery of fifteen presentations. One of these papers highlighted major scientific advances and was produced in collaboration with several other global leaders who attended the Congress of the International Society of Thrombosis and Haemostasis .
Second, the Canadian Cardiovascular Society continued its MYCOVACC surveillance study of myocarditis and pericarditis following mRNA COVID-19 Vaccination, which started in 2022. During 2023–24, they expanded the number of study sites to over 30 across Canada to ensure a nationally representative patient sample for both pediatric and adult patients. They published bilingual infographics for both patients (Information for Patients: Myocarditis and Pericarditis after mRNA Covid-19 Vaccination fact sheet) and healthcare providers (Myocarditis and Pericarditis after mRNA Covid-19 Vaccination - Management Algorithm for Health Care Providers fact sheet )as well as an important paper in the Canadian Journal of Cardiology that includes new evidence about the epidemiology and outcomes of patients with the condition as well as consensus guidance for patient evaluation, management and follow-up.
Four new contribution agreements were formed in 2023–24:
The first is the Canadian National Vaccine Safety Network (CANVAS) at Dalhousie University. This project, titled CIRN CANVAS: Vaccine Active Safety Surveillance, conducts active vaccine safety surveillance for COVID-19, influenza and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) vaccines by estimating the frequency of severe Adverse Events Following Immunization (AEFI).
The second is the Sentinel Practitioner Surveillance Network (SPSN), by the Provincial Health Services Authority of British Columbia. This project, titled Acute Respiratory Illness (ARI) Sentinel Practitioner Surveillance Network (SPSN): Virus and Vaccine Effectiveness Monitoring, monitors COVID-19, influenza and non-influenza respiratory viruses to provide vaccine effectiveness estimates to public health and immunization stakeholders and the Canadian public.
The third is the University of Ottawa's Interdisciplinary Centre for Black Health . This project, titled Perceptions of Vaccine Effectiveness and Immunization Coverage: A Comparative Study of Risk and Protective Factors in Racialized and Ethnic Populations in Canada, examines vaccine effectiveness and immunization coverage in racialized equity-seeking communities. The aim is to increase knowledge of attitudes and behaviours around vaccination within these populations.
The fourth project was with CANImmunize. The project, entitled Funding Extension to Support the Canadian Vaccine Catalogue sustained the (CVC) until March 31, 2024 and assisted with the transition to a national vaccine catalogue.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Indigenous Early Learning and Child Care (IELCC) Transformation Initiative supports the implementation of the co-developed Indigenous Early Learning and Child Care Framework . This framework reflects the unique cultures and priorities of First Nations, Inuit and Métis children across Canada.
This initiative enables greater control in IELCC through a new partnership model to facilitate Indigenous-led decision-making to advance national and regional priorities.
Employment and Social Development Canada (ESDC) is the federal lead for this horizontal initiative while Indigenous Services Canada and PHAC are key partners.
The IELCC Transformation Initiative contributed to the shared objective of providing high quality early learning and childcare services that are also affordable, flexible and inclusive, as outlined in ESDC's Horizontal Management Framework.
The IELCC Transformation Initiative contributed to achieving results through reporting on the number of quality improvement projects funded that, for example, enable the development of curriculum content incorporating Indigenous traditions, cultures and languages; build community, administration, professional capacity and centres of expertise; and support staff training and other activities that will enhance access to high quality IELCC.
Targets will be determined with Indigenous partners.
The IELCC Transformation Initiative will also demonstrate progress on the shared outcome through reporting on the number of children accessing culturally appropriate and inclusive IELCC, with the target to be determined with PHAC (baseline 4,500 children).

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The National Collaborating Centres (NCCs) for Public Health program increases public health system capacity in Canada by improving the ability of research to be applied and implemented in public health settings.
The objective of this program is to promote evidence-informed decision-making by public health practitioners and policy makers across Canada. The NCCs synthesize and share knowledge in ways that are useful and accessible to public health stakeholders.
- Public health partners worked collaboratively to address existing and emerging public health issues;
- Public health organizations participated in collaborative networks and processes; and
- Public health professionals and partners continued to have access to reliable, actionable public health data and information.
In 2023–24, the NCCs for Public Health moved beyond the COVID-19 pandemic response and continued to address ongoing and emerging public health needs such as adapting to climate change, developing the current and future public health workforce in Canada, and mobilizing knowledge on several surveillance priorities related to STTBI, health equity, wastewater, Indigenous health and mathematical modelling. The NCCs contributed to increasing public health capacity by developing a variety of knowledge mobilization tools (e.g., fact sheets, case studies, guidance documents, environmental scans, blogs, disease debriefs, knowledge repositories, rapid reviews, etc.).
- Engaging and collaborating with key public health partners across the country to develop updated public health core competencies aligned with current needs and priorities of the public health system;
- Hosting regional consultations with public health stakeholders in gathering intel to inform the Agency on its future 2030 Vision for Surveillance;
- Highlighting the impacts on health from extreme heat, extreme cold, drought, flooding, and their potential impacts on our air (both indoor and outdoor), water (drinking and recreational) and other aspects of our built environment. These impacts on health include changing patterns of disease-causing vectors due to the warming climate in Canada;
- Assessing the impacts of climate change on food safety and food security, particularly for Indigenous Peoples in Canada;
- Effectively mobilizing expert information on climate change driven health impacts, via national forums, webinar series, social media and through knowledge exchange, serving on provincial and national working group;
- Emphasizing on what public health need to know regarding tuberculosis, HIV, STTBI and population mobility; and
- Implementing and developing the Canadian Network for Health in All Policies.
The NCCs mobilized knowledge by applying their expertise to a wide range of public health areas including:
- Infectious diseases;
- Anti-Black racism and public health;
- Public health organizational and leadership capacity;
- Antimicrobial use and resistance;
- Climate Change and Indigenous Peoples in Canada;
- Determinants of First Nations, Inuit and Métis Peoples' Health;
- Evidence-informed decision-making tools; and
- Intersectoral action and public policy.
The NCCs also undertook knowledge translation activities to support the publication and dissemination and promotion of the 2023 Chief Public Health Officer of Canada's Report on the State of Public Health in Canada .
In 2023–24, the NCCs developed and disseminated over 1,214 products, including published materials, videos, workshops, webinars, online courses, conference presentations and activities which supported practitioners and decision-makers in applying new knowledge in their environments. In addition, the NCCs undertook over 374 knowledge-related needs and gaps identification activities to provide public health knowledge brokers with the resources and structures required to strengthen evidence-informed decision-making.
The NCCs also engaged and maintained over 89 partnerships and collaborations, facilitating and increasing public health outreach and exchange.
Performance indicators include:
- The number and types of activities undertaken that identify research knowledge gaps;
- The number and types of products and activities created and disseminated; and
- The number of collaborations to address emerging public health issues.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this program is to implement and expand wastewater testing capacity for SARS-CoV-2 surveillance. Wastewater-based surveillance of infectious diseases is beyond the standard complement of program funding for PHAC. The contribution will provide funding for partners in the wastewater surveillance network to collect and test samples (within communities or at PHAC) for pathogens such as SARS-CoV-2. Wastewater testing can provide an early warning signal to inform public health action (e.g., vaccination programs, masking).
The National Wastewater Monitoring Program supported implementation of wastewater surveillance across provinces and territories, including surveillance projects at borders and airports. In fiscal year 2023–24, the program continued to build testing capacity for COVID-10, influenza (the flu), and RSV across 68 community sites across Canada. In fiscal years 2022–23 and 2023–24, PHAC established 27 contribution agreements to address several priorities: to increase wastewater testing and enhance or expand wastewater monitoring capacity in provinces, territories and Indigenous communities across Canada; to increase surveillance at border and airports; to build on the necessary research and development foundation required for the timely detection and surveillance of COVID-19 and develop assays for other priority pathogens. To help establish testing capacity across Canada, a wastewater testing hub was established in Atlantic Canada and wastewater surveillance activities were implemented in partnership with First Nations communities. PHAC launched a public-facing dashboard on Infobase to inform individual and public health decision-making for COVID-19, which contributed to knowledge mobilization and interpretation of results for public health authorities. In 2023–24 and in collaboration with partners, PHAC has included COVID-19 variant tracking information from wastewater monitoring on the national wastewater dashboard and continued to add new sites to the wastewater SARS-CoV-2 variants page, with 52 sites currently being published. Capacity built for COVID-19 was leveraged for mpox surveillance to support Canada's response to the outbreak. The program continued to support ongoing research on expanding surveillance activities to include other high-priority pathogens and targets such as mpox, polio, highly pathogenic avian influenza and AMR targets.

#### Preventing and Addressing Family Violence: The Health Perspective
Type of transfer payment: Grants and Contributions
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Preventing and Addressing Family Violence: The Health Perspective program invests in the delivery and testing of health promotion programs and interventions that prevent family violence and improve health outcomes for survivors of family violence including intimate partner violence, child maltreatment and mistreatment of older persons, also known as elder abuse.
The objectives of the program include increasing the evidence base and uptake of health promotion programs and interventions that are effective in preventing and addressing family violence and its health impacts using trauma and violence-informed approaches. This program is part of the Government of Canada's Strategy to Prevent and Address Gender-Based Violence .
In 2023–24, PHAC funded 35 projects, including four grants, under the Preventing and Addressing Family Violence: The Health Perspective Investment. Of these, 27 are community-based projects delivering and testing health promotion programs and interventions aimed at supporting the mental and physical health of those who have experienced, are experiencing, or at risk of experiencing family violence. For example:
- The DIVERSECity Community Resources Society is adapting and delivering a multigenerational safety planning system to address and prevent family violence with diverse cultural communities in Surrey, British Columbia;
- Carleton University is delivering and evaluating a trauma-and violence-informed physical activity program to 225 women in three geographically and culturally diverse sites in Ottawa, Toronto, and Vancouver; and
- The University of Calgary is adapting, delivering and testing the Alberta Men's Network Training Program to prevent and address domestic violence in Black communities in Calgary and Toronto.
Six projects, including three new agreements signed in 2023–24, are building the capacity of professional and service provider to identify and safely respond to family violence through guidance and training. For example:
- iHEAL is a free, private and secure app that helps Canadian women who have experienced abuse from a current or past partner find personalized ways to stay safe and be well. It was developed by researchers at the University of Western Ontario, University of British Columbia, and University of New Brunswick, was launched in November 2023.
Two funded projects are communities of practice that connect practitioners and researchers across funded projects, building research and practice capacity, as well as enhancing knowledge mobilization. For example:
- The Central Toronto Youth Services is developing and leading Tending the Roots, for professionals and service providers from diverse organizations across Canada who are doing family-focused work with 2SLGBTQI+ children and youth.
In 2023–24, funded projects under this investment reached over 1,500 participants including over 300 children or youth, 1,200 adults and 40 older adults. They also reached over 1,300 professionals and service providers working with families, survivors and those at higher risk of experiencing family violence. These projects also developed 75 new knowledge products, such as videos (including recorded webinars), research summaries and reports, guidance resources and infographics. They also held 61 new knowledge mobilization events, including webinars, presentations and workshops.
At this early stage in projects' activities and reporting, available numbers show that a majority of participants report acquiring knowledge and skills, and applying these skills in their lives. Projects working to build the capacity of service providers and professionals report very high levels of satisfaction with the resources and training, with nearly all professionals acquiring knowledge and skills and a majority applying them in their professional practice.

The Preventing Gender-Based Violence: the Health Perspective Program invests in the delivery and testing of health promotion programs to prevent teen dating violence and child maltreatment and equips health and social service professionals to respond safely and effectively to gender-based violence. This program is part of the Government of Canada's Strategy to Prevent and Address Gender-Based Violence .
Program objectives include supporting the delivery and evaluation of diverse initiatives, developing and sharing knowledge of effective approaches to prevent child maltreatment and dating violence among teens/youth, as well as equipping health and allied professionals to recognize, prevent and respond safely and effectively to gender-based violence.
In 2023–24, PHAC continued to support funding agreements with 32 projects through the Preventing Gender-Based Violence: The Health Perspective Program, including two grants. This program includes projects to prevent youth dating violence and child maltreatment, and to build the capacity of professionals to safely respond to gender-based violence. PHAC also funds the Promoting Relationships and Eliminating Violence Network (PREVNet) to facilitate a community of practice (CoP) that connects teen dating violence prevention projects to amplify outcomes through networking, capacity building, and knowledge mobilization.
- In Nova Scotia, the Antigonish Women's Resource Centre and Sexual Assault Services Association is delivering and evaluating a peer-facilitated school-based violence prevention program called Healthy Relationships for Youth. Delivered to Grade 9 students primarily in rural-based communities including Mi'Kmaq and African Nova Scotian communities, and newcomers to Canada, the program is helping students build and maintain healthy relationships;
- Positive Discipline in Everyday Life, an organization based out of Winnipeg, Manitoba, is delivering and evaluating the Positive Discipline in Everyday Parenting (PDEP) program aimed at changing parental beliefs and emotional reactions known to precipitate physical and emotional punishment of children. The program is reaching parents and guardians among diverse communities in Canada, including Indigenous families, newcomers, young parents and guardians, grandparents, parents involved in the child welfare system, and parents and guardians with low literacy; and
- The Coaching Association of Canada is developing, implementing and evaluating a bilingual intervention to assist coaches in recognizing, preventing and addressing gender-based violence and teen dating violence, and promoting healthy relationships in and through sport. The resources support approximately 6,000 coaches across Canada to address issues such as bystander empowerment, men and boys as allies in the prevention of violence, and locker room gender-based violence.
The 2023–24 fiscal year was the final year of funding for most projects, not all of which were in operation for the full year. During that time, funded projects delivered programming to over 6,400 participants, including over 5,800 children and youth, as well as over 18,000 service providers and professionals. Projects also developed 300 new knowledge products and hosted over 200 knowledge events, reaching a total audience of over 60,000. Project participants reported increased skills and knowledge relating to healthy relationships and positive parenting, as well as positive changes in behaviour when they put these skills to use. Projects working with service providers reported very high satisfaction with the trainings and resources provided, as well as increased capacity to implement trauma-informed practices. The evidence from effective interventions continued to inform policies and programs.
PHAC also launched new solicitations under the Preventing Gender-Based Violence: The Health Perspective investment in the summer and fall of 2023, drawing on stakeholders' expertise and the latest evidence. These solicitations help address evidence gaps and needs in the prevention of youth dating violence, the prevention of child maltreatment, and professionals' capacity to safely recognize and respond to gender-based violence, with a focus on reaching underserved populations in culturally safe and trauma-informed ways. Funded projects will start over the course of fiscal year 2024–25.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this program is to gain a better understanding of COVID-19 and the degree of immunity to COVID-19 to inform Canadian and global public health science, policy and health care responses.
The Sero-Surveillance and Research Program was developed to administer the COVID-19 Immunity Task Force (CITF), which was established by the Government of Canada in April 2020, with an investment of $300 million. This investment, initially over two fiscal years, was extended to four years as a result of the ongoing pandemic. The program coordinated national serological studies, vaccine surveillance studies, and immunity studies across Canada and in sub-populations, with unique or high-risk profiles. PHAC managed the funding and administration of these studies through Grants and Contributions based on recommendations by governance committees.
The CITF program funded 120 studies across all provinces and territories in Canada, both in the general populations and specific age, risk, and other demographic groups, including racialized and marginalized communities. The CITF mobilized studies and collected data in many different equity-deserving populations, including children and older persons, Indigenous communities, those living in correctional facilities and in long-term care homes, those experiencing housing insecurity, those working high risk occupations (e.g. front-line food workers), those from 2SLGBTQIA+ communities and those at higher risk due to other health conditions.
The studies included areas such as immune science, seroprevalence, vaccine safety and effectiveness, and immune testing. A defining strength of the CITF was the ability to assemble and mobilize an abundance of incredibly talented and committed persons across Canada as a "call to arms" for emergency science.
CITF-funded researchers generated over 335 academic articles (evergreen) and preprints related to CITF-funded projects. Results helped to determine the extent of SARS-CoV-2 infection and immune response in Canada and within high-risk subgroups. The CITF generated research to inform evidence-based decisions related to the COVID-19 pandemic response. CITF serosurveys provided the only nationally representative picture of seroprevalence for COVID-19 in Canada. Insights contributed to a greater understanding of the safety and effectiveness of vaccines across different sub-populations and the extent of immune protection arising from infection and vaccination. This informed public health policy and decision-making in various ways, including NACI recommendations.

#### Strengthening the Canadian Drugs and Substances Strategy (Harm Reduction Fund)

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Canadian Drugs and Substances Strategy (CDSS) is the Government of Canada's comprehensive, collaborative, compassionate and evidence-based approach to drug policy, which uses a public health lens when considering and addressing substance use. The CDSS is led by the Minister of Mental Health and Addictions and Associate Minister of Health, supported by Health Canada and 14 other federal departments and agencies. The CDSS covers a broad range of legal and illegal substances, including cannabis, alcohol, opioids and other substances. The goal of the strategy is to protect the health and safety of all people living in Canada by minimizing harms from substance use for individuals, families and communities.
The CDSS recognizes that a national approach to substance use requires strong collaboration and coordination among all levels of government, working in their respective areas of jurisdiction. It also recognizes the critical importance of working closely with a wide range of stakeholders on an ongoing basis, including people with lived and living experience with substance use. Reducing the stigma experienced by people who use drugs is also integral to the CDSS and an ongoing priority for the Government of Canada.
As a partner department, PHAC receives contributions under the CDSS to support federal harm reduction efforts through the Harm Reduction Fund (HRF). Through the HRF, PHAC complements provincial and territorial harm reduction strategies by investing $7 million annually to support time-limited projects across Canada to help reduce HIV and hepatitis C among people who share injection and inhalation drug-use equipment. Harm reduction refers to a set of practices that aim to reduce the harms associated with substance use. Harm reduction:
- is about meeting people where they are at and supporting them to work towards their goals;
- aims to decrease adverse health, social and economic outcomes, such as disease and injury that may result from an individual's actions;
- represents policies, strategies, services and practices, which aim to assist people to live safer and healthier lives;
- acknowledges that each person is different, has different goals and requires different supports and strategies; and
- is not focused on the reduction of substance use or abstinence as a precursor to receive respect, compassion or services.
In 2023–24, PHAC provided $7 million through the HRF to fund 40 community-based projects that address HIV, hepatitis C, and other sexually transmitted and blood-borne infections. While projects are still currently reporting on their impact from 2023–24, preliminary data suggests that approximately 55,000 individuals who use and share inhalation or injection drug-use equipment and 2,900 individuals in key populations were reached in 2022– 23 across the country.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The objectives of this program are to:
- Promote mental health and prevent mental illness in populations most affected by the COVID-19 pandemic;
- Build evidence regarding effective interventions to promote mental health and prevent mental illness in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic and post-pandemic recovery; and
- Enhance capacity of individuals, service providers and organizations to promote mental health and prevent mental illness in safe, effective and trauma-informed ways.
In 2023–24, PHAC continued its focus on mental health by supporting 54 projects under the Supporting the Mental Health of Those Most Affected by COVID-19 investment. These projects are delivering and evaluating mental health promotion and prevention interventions, thereby addressing the needs of key populations disproportionately impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic. The projects focus on resilience building, peer support, mental health literacy and providing training and resources, to build capacity of service providers and beneficiaries in addressing the new and complex mental health needs that emerged or were amplified due to the pandemic. Examples of initiatives funded through this investment include:
- A program reaching youth in classrooms to build mental health literacy and skills for help-seeking;
- A project that is using "social prescribing" to refer individuals to social and community supports that promote mental health; and
- A parenting support program to enhance parent and child mental health.
Of the 54 funded projects, 36 projects have ended in the 2023–24 fiscal year. As of March 31, 2024, preliminary findings indicate that funded projects have reached over 2,424,699 individuals across more than 910 sites through capacity building and knowledge exchange activities.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose and objective of the program is the development and real-time distribution of antimicrobial prescribing guidelines to eligible Canadian health professionals for evidence-based prescribing, and as a decision-making tool to minimize inappropriate or unnecessary use of antimicrobials. Another objective is the development of an enhanced prescribing monitoring program protocol for collecting data on antimicrobial prescribing practices in Canadian hospitals and other settings that provide health care previously piloted in Canada.
The Association of Medical Microbiology and Infectious Disease Canada (AMMI Canada) has created a working group to develop national antimicrobial prescribing guidelines for certain infectious diseases. The working group has national representation from experts, including physicians and pharmacists specialized in the field. Using a methodological framework, the WHO AWaRe Antibiotic Handbook will be evaluated and either adopted, adapted, or rewritten, in the development of evidence informed Canadian guidelines.
Spectrum (Firstline), which developed an online clinical decision support platform, established a partnership with both AMMI Canada and PHAC to support dissemination of the national antimicrobial prescribing guidelines. The Firstline digital platform is being configured for this project, which included security role and authorization definitions, content subtypes and branding. Access to the platform has been granted to working group administrators and the user guide and supporting documentation has been imported. Firstline staff have been assigned to continue managing the partnerships and project outputs.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Blood Safety Contribution Program (BSCP) supports the development and/or enhancement of provincial and territorial systems that monitor adverse events associated with the transfusion of blood and blood products and the transplantation of Cells, Tissues and Organs (CTOs). The recipients of BSCP funding include provincial and territorial governments, transfusion and transplantation centres, agencies or groups designated by provincial and territorial Ministries of Health to undertake surveillance for blood, tissue, organ-associated adverse events and Canadian not-for profit organizations that support transfusion and transplantation adverse event surveillance activities. Recipients of the funding then transfer information to PHAC via BSCP's Transfusion Error Surveillance System (TESS), Transfusion-Transmitted Injuries Surveillance System (TTISS) and Cells, Tissues and Organs Surveillance System (CTOSS). This information is prepared, analyzed and reported at a national level and is made available to recipients and other stakeholders of the transfusion and transplantation system. This information is used to identify adverse event and error trends, to benchmark provincial/territorial adverse events against national-level data and to make international comparisons. The surveillance information has also contributed to the development of transfusion guidelines to improve transfusion practices.
BSCP projects establish systems to monitor adverse events associated with transfusion and transplantation that could involve infectious diseases. As the need and use of blood, blood products and CTOs continue to increase in Canada, there is an elevated risk of adverse events. Monitoring adverse events will allow for more timely response in the event of a new or previously unknown blood and/or CTO safety issue.
In partnership with Canadian sentinel hospitals, the Centre for Communicable Diseases and Infection Control (CCDIC) is responsible for the collection, management and analysis of the TTISS, TESS and CTOSS data as well as the production of reports summarizing key findings. These data and products are used to inform public health and policy action and support continuous improvement of PHAC's surveillance standards.
In 2023–2024, the Blood Safety Contribution Program (BSCP) published the Transfusion Transmitted Injuries Surveillance System Summary Report, 2016 to 2020 that summarized 2016–20 trends and the 2020-2021 surveillance data on transfusion transmitted injuries report.

#### Canadian Immunization Research Network
Type of transfer payment: Grants
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Canadian Immunization Research Network (CIRN) was established in 2014, having transitioned from the influenza specific PHAC/Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR) Influenza Research Network. The CIRN is a multidisciplinary 'network of networks' that addresses diverse immunization research requirements in Canada on topics related to safety, efficacy, effectiveness, uptake, program delivery and evaluation. The CIRN is composed of eight research sub-networks, with approximately 200 investigators across 67 Canadian institutions. The CIRN is a key element of Canada's public health emergency response infrastructure, functioning in a research readiness capacity.
In 2023–24, the CIRN delivered on its objectives by continuing to support infrastructure to facilitate collaborative research among vaccine and immunization researchers, clinicians, public health professionals and policy makers to develop methodologies to test vaccines, evaluate immunization programs, improve coverage rates, train researchers and improve knowledge exchange. Areas of research supported through this initiative included evaluation of vaccines for safety and immunogenicity, population-based methods for vaccine effectiveness and safety, as well as interventions that improve vaccine acceptance and uptake, modelling and economic analysis.
Research supported by the CIRN explored priority policy areas, including sex and gender considerations; research on Indigenous populations and Official Language Minority Communities; ethical, legal and social implications; and the importance of leveraging existing clinical trials and using a variety of methodologies inclusive of other clinical trials.
In 2023–24, the CIRN's eight sub networks were engaged in supporting Canada's COVID-19 vaccine readiness and response through supplementary funding provided for this purpose. Research was conducted in COVID-19 vaccine clinical trials readiness, vaccine safety and effectiveness, population prioritization and modelling and vaccine acceptance and uptake. The CIRN also coordinated data collection and information-sharing with knowledge users through its well-established strategies and methods.
In 2023–24, a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) was signed by the Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR) and PHAC to develop and launch the funding opportunity for a renewed CIRN. The CIRN renewal is a directed grant of $3 million per year that will be administered via CIHR for five years, until 2028–29. The funding objectives and research areas of focus were supported by PHAC.

#### Combating Antibiotic-Resistance Bacteria (CARB-X)
Type of transfer payment: Grants

- Result 2.1: Infectious diseases are prevented and controlled
Link to the department's program inventory:

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
CARB-X is a leading global organization whose mission is to drive a diverse portfolio of innovative antibacterial products towards clinical development. CARB-X fills a critical gap to help stem the rise of AMR by focusing both on the most serious drug-resistant bacteria identified by the WHO and the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), as well as on the syndromes with the highest degrees of mortality and morbidity.
CARB-X is currently funded by a global consortium of governments and foundations, including the United States, the United Kingdom, Germany, the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation and Wellcome Trust . It is also recognized by world leaders, including by G7 Health Ministers in their 2023 Communiqué and G20 Health Ministers in their 2023 Outcome Document .
Funding from Canada will support CARB-X's efforts in replenishing the global clinical pipeline with high-impact products which the world, including Canada, needs to address AMR in the years to come.
By providing funding to CARB-X, Canada is contributing to global AMR innovation by increasing funding to a global pooled fund that accelerates development of innovative solutions around the world.
This project is intended to increase scientific, regulatory and business support to product developers of diagnostics, therapeutics and preventatives around the globe who are focused on the most serious drug-resistant bacteria identified by the WHO and the Center for Disease Control (CDC), and on the syndromes with the highest degrees of mortality and morbidity.
This funding allowed Canada to benefit from the successful outcomes of CARB-X investments in new antimicrobial drugs and to become part of a partnership of co-founders and innovators who are leading the global AMR innovation agenda. PHAC invested $6.3 million in CARB-X in 2023. Additionally, Canada provided seed funding to the Global Antibiotic Research and Development Partnership's (GARD-P) SECURE initiative to expand equitable access of antibiotics around the world. Of the $6.3 million, $6 million was a grant to CARB-X to contribute to global AMR innovation by strengthening the discovery or development of new antimicrobial drugs while $300,000 was to provide a survey of the Canadian landscape, for which the summary report was received in April 2024.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this program was to provide evidence-based, interactive individual risk assessment tools that will help people in Canada make informed decisions about gathering or visiting with others in a way that reduces their risk of transmission of COVID-19 and other respiratory infectious diseases.
The objective was to support Toronto Metropolitan University's National Institute on Ageing (NIA) to develop a shorter and more user-friendly COVID-19 visit risk tool, My COVID-19 Visit Risk Calculator , while retaining the more comprehensive tool, My COVID-19 Visit Risk Decision Aid , with revisions and enhancements. This work was done to ensure the tools accurately reflected existing guidance and advice on individual public health measures and ongoing promotional materials.
Funding will continue to support ongoing maintenance (e.g., revisions to reflect emerging evidence), enhancements to the tools, development of promotional campaigns and enhanced reporting capacities. In 2022–23, objectives were expanded to account for updates to tool content specific to the emerging Omicron variant of concern and ongoing community transmission of COVID-19 at that time. In 2023–24, the objectives of the tools were again expanded to include content and considerations specific to RSV and influenza.
Results associated with the tools include the following:
- Development of the My COVID-19 Visit Risk Calculator as a shorter, more user-friendly version of NIA's previous tool, My COVID-19 Visit Risk Decision Aid;
- Content revisions for both tools to ensure they align with national COVID-19 Public Health Measures guidance and recommendations, as well as improved accessibility;
- Updated content and enhancements were made for the Omicron variant of concern and ongoing transmission of COVID-19 during the pandemic based on the best available evidence;
- Updates made to include content specific to RSV and influenza, including risk factors and considerations specific to those diseases;
- The launch of seasonal public awareness campaigns to increase the profile and uptake of the tools, resulting in 1,875,798 web page views as of March 2024, with an increasing proportion of users outside of Canada, such as the United States and Mexico; and
- The tool has garnered 39 media mentions (e.g. New York Times, CTV, National Post) as of March 2024.
