Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
This funding program supports community-based projects intended to improve the wellbeing of people living with dementia and their caregivers and to increase general knowledge about risk and protective factors. The program also provides funding support for the Canadian Dementia Learning and Resource Network (CDLRN), a knowledge hub led by Schlegel-UW Research Institute for Aging to facilitate collaboration among Dementia Community Investment (DCI) projects and shares lessons learned with the broader community.
PHAC completed a fall 2022 solicitation, which led to the funding of six new projects in 2023. These projects are focused on improving the wellbeing of people living with dementia and their caregivers, and increasing knowledge about risk and protective factors. To date, 31 projects have been funded through the Dementia Community Investment, 10 of which are currently in progress. Program participants have gained resources, knowledge, and skills to provide enhanced support to people living with dementia and to support their own wellbeing.
The performance indicators and outcomes contained in final reports from Dementia Community Investment projects received in spring 2023 are:
- 90% of program participants reported increased knowledge or skills because of programming. This data, collected from 13 projects, reflects responses from 1,545 out of 1,725 participants;
- 83% of program participants reported improved changes in behaviour as a result of the programming. This data, collected from 10 projects, reflects responses from 1,752 out of 2,129 participants;
- 69% of program participants reported improved changes in protective factors because of programming. This data, collected from 10 projects, reflects responses from 685 out of 990 participants.;
- 57% of program participants reported improved changes in wellbeing because of programming. This data, collected from 10 projects, reflects responses from 748 out of 1,319 participants; and
- The CDLRN knowledge hub, funded through DCI, developed a dementia interventions playbook in 2024 that will allow individuals and organizations to learn more about the work, successes and the products created by DCI recipients.

A study was conducted by the Immunization Monitoring Program ACTive (IMPACT) research network, administered by the Canadian Paediatric Society, to advance understanding on Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) hospitalizations, detect which populations are experiencing surges in RSV cases and determine which at-risk communities would benefit most from RSV countermeasures.
Results of the study of novel data to understand the burden of RSV disease during the 2022–23 respiratory season in Canadian children was collected from 13 tertiary care centres. This was the first season in which RSV, the flu and COVID-19 co-circulated. Through this work, populations experiencing surges in RSV cases were identified and information on how these diseases' transmission dynamics differed among the pediatric population was reported, as well as differences in epidemiology pre-and post-pandemic. This included information on populations who may benefit most from RSV countermeasures.
PHAC received a report with preliminary findings (IMPACT Population-based Surveillance of Respiratory Syncytial Virus Infections at Participating Centers across Canada for 2022–23) in fall 2023 outlining trends that were observed in the 2022–23 respiratory virus season and were used to inform expected trends for the 2023–24 season. A final report is to be provided to PHAC and subsequently published by the Canadian Pediatric Society, making this information available to researchers, public health professionals, infection control specialists and physicians to inform decision-making for upcoming respiratory virus seasons.

#### Emerging Respiratory Illness Issues – External Grants Program (focused on evidence-based public health guidance and advice)
Type of transfer payment: Grants
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2023–24
Link to departmental result(s):
- Result 2.1: Infectious diseases are prevented and controlled
Link to the department's Program Inventory:
- Communicable Disease and Infection Control
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this program is to fund research that will fill knowledge gaps in scientific evidence to inform public health measures guidance and advice to people living in Canada regarding respiratory infectious diseases (such as COVID-19, the flu and RSV). This will help ensure that Canadian recommendations are based on current evidence thereby contributing to a stronger, more informed and more resilient public health system. Priority topics for each solicitation are identified through consultations and by aligning with national and international research priorities. Previous objectives included funding projects focused on investigating the effectiveness of public health measures for COVID-19 and those that examine the transmission dynamics of respiratory infectious diseases, including COVID-19, the flu and RSV.
A call for Letters of Intent (LOIs) was launched in July 2023. A total of 42 letters were received and a total of $752,338 was awarded to the following four projects:
- Investigating genomic variability and phylodynamics of respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) (Queens University at Kingston);
- Comprehensive studies of indoor air quality and its relationship with airborne RID transmission "hot-spots" in long-term care facilities (Carleton University);
- Estimating viral respiratory pathogen shedding for eight pathogens (including influenza, RSV, SARS-CoV-2) based on RT-PCR cycle thresholds (IRS-SHED) (Ontario Agency for Health Protection and Promotion); and
- School-based interventions to mitigate the impact of the tripledemic: Simulation-based evidence to guide high-impact public health and environmental measures (University of Waterloo). Since there are few Canadian studies published on the transmission dynamics of COVID-19, the flu, and RSV, the outcomes from this research are expected to inform federal policy regarding public health measures for respiratory infectious diseases. Outcomes will also contribute Canadian data to the body of public health measures evidence available to policy makers globally.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
This program will support partnerships with community-based organizations, research institutions and provincial and territorial governments to support projects that promote national education and awareness, prevention of alcohol use in pregnancy, improvement of health and social outcomes for those affected by Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder (FASD) and research into the social determinants of health that impact alcohol consumption and FASD. The FASD National Strategic Projects Fund supported five contribution agreements in 2023–24, including two new agreements. These projects support the prevention of FASD and reduction of stigma associated with the syndrome. The projects continued to reach an audience of non-pregnant women and people of childbearing age, pregnant women and people and their partners, young adults, people with FASD, service providers, and policy makers.
In 2023–24, the project activities included:

- Increasing collaborative action and networking amongst diverse stakeholders related to FASD prevention, awareness and capacity building, including through delivering an Atlantic conference, "Reducing Harms in Atlantic Communities: Synthesizing Voices in Health Equity, FASD, and Trauma," with speakers, presenters, organizers and attendees from across the Atlantic region;

- Developing a social media and radio ad campaign focused on FASD prevention and awareness, which is also available in Mi'kmaq; and
- Hosting consultations across the country to support a comprehensive scientific assessment on FASD with the aim to strengthen PHAC's approach to address FASD and inform future priorities for action.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
This program supports communities to develop comprehensive, culturally and linguistically appropriate programs to improve the health and development of children (0 to 6 years). The program also intends to improve access to early childhood health promotion programming for children and their families living in Official Language Minority Communities (OLMC) and is an initiative under the 2018–2023 Action Plan for Official Languages .
In 2023–24, the two Healthy Early Years (HEY) program funding recipients funded 66 third party projects aimed at improving access for vulnerable families living in OLMCs to early childhood health promotion programming, with the goal of helping these populations acquire knowledge and skills, adopt positive healthy behaviours and improve long-term health outcomes.
A variety of public health topics were addressed and promoted through funded projects, with a focus on mental health of parents, guardians and families and healthy child development. In 2023–24, 17 of the 66 funded projects received additional, one-time funding, to adapt components of their program activities specifically focused on children on the autism spectrum and their families living in Official Language Minority Communities (OLMC) across Canada.
In addition, over 592 new partnerships were created because of HEY program funding.
In 2023–24, approximately 22,000 participants (children aged 0 to 6 years and parents and guardians) benefited from the program.
Survey data collected in 2021–22 found that 77% of program participants gained knowledge and skills because of attending the program, 88% reported improved health behaviours and 73% reported that their health and wellbeing improved because of attending the program.

#### Infectious Diseases and Climate Change Fund - Adapting to the Impacts of Climate Change
Type of transfer payment: Grants and Contributions Footnote 5
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018
Link to departmental result:
- Result 2.1: Infectious diseases are prevented and controlled
Link to the department's program inventory:
- Foodborne and Zoonotic Diseases
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Infectious Disease and Climate Change Fund (IDCCF) focuses on preparing and protecting people living in Canada from climate-sensitive infectious diseases that are zoonotic, foodborne and waterborne. This includes actions that stimulate public health innovation using a One Health approach by linking human, animal and environmental health and in advancing climate change adaptation.
The IDCCF addresses the impact of climate change on human health in Canada by:
- Increasing capacity to understand and respond to the rising demands posed by climate-sensitive infectious diseases; and
- Equipping health professionals and people living in Canada with timely and accurate information to better understand their risks and take measures to prevent infection.
The IDCCF focuses on the following activities for Monitoring and Surveillance:
- Building baseline data and enhancing knowledge and expertise to understand, predict and monitor current and future risks through innovative approaches to surveillance, detection and analysis of climate-sensitive infectious diseases; and
- Developing collaborative and novel approaches for the collection, sharing and use of data to support evidence-based public health actions that equip and empower people living in Canada to adapt.
The following activities focus on Education and Awareness:
- Promoting the development, distribution and uptake of education and awareness materials for health professionals; and
- Facilitating education, awareness and the dissemination of tools and best practices within or across Canadian communities and among vulnerable populations.
In 2023–24, the Infectious Disease and Climate Change Fund established six new funding agreements that aim to better protect at-risk populations, equip and empower the next generation of public health professionals, leverage citizen science approaches and advance One Health to build community resilience to climate change. The new projects will build baseline data, enhance monitoring and increase our understanding of new or emerging tick-borne and mosquito-borne diseases. They will also increase health professional capacity through the development of health tools, training and better resources to improve understanding of the origin of various infectious diseases and the impact of climate change, as well as the effect on the health of people living in Canada to improve the prediction and prevention of illnesses.
Since launching in 2017, the IDCCF has invested in 41 projects that support surveillance and monitoring, health professional education, and public awareness activities related to climate-driven infectious diseases in Canada.

#### Integrated Strategy for Healthy Living and Chronic Disease – Enhanced Surveillance for Chronic Disease
Type of transfer payment: Grants and Contributions
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.3: Chronic diseases are prevented
Link to the department's program inventory:
- Evidence for Health Promotion, and Chronic Disease and Injury Prevention
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this program is to enhance capacity for public health chronic disease surveillance activities to expand data sources, address persistent public health surveillance evidence gaps and support the development of a robust evidence base on chronic diseases and conditions, injuries, problematic substance use and their risk factors in Canada.
Through the Enhanced Surveillance for Chronic Disease Program (ESCDP), 12 contribution agreements totalling $10.8 million over four years were signed in 2019. Projects funded through this initiative explored the use of new technologies, tools and approaches to collect, use and disseminate data for public health use. This cycle of ESCDP focused on priority populations (e.g., 2SLGBTQIA+, people without homes, ethnic minorities, older adults and those experiencing lower socio-economic conditions) and the built environment's influence on population health and chronic disease factors. Notable results included:
First, the New Horizons Indigenous Association (NHIA) co-developed a culturally-safe survey tool with the Chief and leaders of Gwa'sala-'Nakwaxda'xw (GNN) to provide the community with meaningful data regarding chronic illness in its population and build an understanding of the health of the community. This community-based research model, which integrated GNN's cultural values and leveraged academic partnerships, set the foundation to empower the community to be fully engaged with research focused on the health of its people. As an example, the project's blended Ethics Approval model prioritized the voices and perspectives of GNN, respecting their cultural integrity and autonomy. In addition, with support from Vancouver Island University and Island Health, NHIA trained members of the GNN community to administer the culturally-safe survey to members of their community. The success of this project funded by PHAC enhanced collaboration with other partners towards the long-term sustainability of community-driven research initiatives.
Second, a ground-breaking national health surveillance system on the health of people who are experiencing or have experienced incarceration, a population often overseen in chronic disease surveillance efforts, was developed by researchers at McMaster University in collaboration with Correctional Service Canada (CSC). This initiative, which included input from individuals with lived or living experience of incarceration, other academics in the field, and representatives from varied community organizations, facilitated the exchange of resources, enabled research to be conducted on varied stakeholder needs, and equipped CSC with case definition algorithms for use with their own electronic medical record data.
Third, the Institut national de santé publique du Québec (INSPQ) developed a monitoring system that integrates road safety issues and travel patterns influenced by the built environment and their impact on health. The information generated by these monitoring activities can support decision-making to improve road safety and encourage physical activity and the use of active modes of transport through actions on the built environment.
Fourth, the Our Health study by the Community Based Research Centre (CBRC) was also completed with the overarching goal of conducting and optimizing community-based strategies for a Canada-wide online and in-person chronic health survey of 2S/LGBTQQIA+ people. Outputs related to this project are made available in English and French on the CBRC website , which include a dynamic data visualization dashboard of key study variables.

#### Integrated Strategy for Healthy Living and Chronic Disease – Pan-Canadian Joint Consortium for School Health
Type of transfer payment: Grants

Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Pan-Canadian Joint Consortium for School Health (JCSH) grant was established in 2005 to support the health, well-being and achievement of children and youth in school settings, and brings together ministries responsible for health and education from most provinces and territories. The grant supports the JCSH Secretariat (through the Government of Prince Edward Island), which facilitates collaboration among provincial and territorial members to promote wellness and achievement in Canada's school-aged children and youth by way of a Comprehensive School Health approach. The Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC) serves as the federal representative on the JCSH and acts in an advisor and observer capacity. The School Health Grant for Youth Program provides youth aged 13 to 19 and enrolled in grades 9 to 12 (or Secondary 3 to 5 in Québec) with the opportunity to apply for small grant funding to develop youth-led initiatives that encourage and promote healthy living in their school. Youth projects must align with the following PHAC priorities: substance use and related harms, positive mental health and well-being, healthy eating and nutrition and physical activity. The Program's first open solicitation was launched in 2023–24 and was preceded by two targeted solicitations in 2022–23. Funding for the School Health Grant for Youth comes from existing A-Base resources for the JCSH.
Joint Consortium for School Health
In 2023–24, the JCSH was in the third year of its four-year grant mandate awarded in April 2021. Grant funding from PHAC provides funding to support the operating costs of the JCSH Secretariat until 2025. The JCSH consists of the Secretariat and two committees: the School Health Coordinators Committee (SHCC),which consists of officials from participating provincial and territorial education and health ministries, and serves as a pan-Canadian forum to support collaboration and alignment between health and educator sectors in the promotion of student success and well-being, and the Management Committee, which consists of senior executive-level participants and provides the main forum for executive level discussion and decisions affecting the work of the JCSH. With grant funding in place, the JCSH Secretariat convened several meetings of these committees, along with joint Management Committee-SHCC meetings and webinar events, which are an opportunity to hear from researchers, policy makers and other key players in the field of school-aged child and youth health, and to learn about latest research findings and initiatives.
The JCSH Secretariat organized, convened and led seven Task Groups, whose objectives were to carry out the activities outlined in the grant mandate workplan. These Task Groups were as follows: Annual Workplan, Evaluation, Equity, Health Behaviour in School-aged Children Research Advisory Committee, Healthy School Planner, Reconciliation, and Research Network. In addition, the JCSH also shared knowledge and information through the distribution of numerous News Bundles and environmental scans from various jurisdictions. These activities contributed to information sharing, knowledge exchange, and capacity building among JCSH member jurisdictions. The JCSH also administered a contract with researchers at the University of Alberta to develop a new resource, Canadian Standards and Indicators for Health Promoting Schools . This resource has been completed, following intensive research, consultation with education and health experts throughout the country, and collaboration of the research leads and a JCSH advisory team.
School Health Grant for Youth
Through the School Health Grant for Youth Program, PHAC awarded grant funding to 81 youth recipients in fiscal year 2023–24. This grant Program provided youth with funding to develop and implement youth-led projects to support healthy living in schools. Participating youth gained knowledge, tools, and resources to support healthy living, and youth funding recipients gained valuable life skills such as leadership, project planning and giving back to the community. Youth-led projects all focused on one or a combination of the grant's four priority areas, with the majority of youth projects focusing on positive mental health and well-being.

#### Mental Health Promotion Innovation Fund
Type of transfer payment: Grants and Contributions
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates

- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
- Result 1.2: Canadians have improved health behaviours
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Mental Health Promotion Innovation Fund (MHPIF) is a funding program that replaced the Innovation Strategy in 2019–20 to support positive mental health for children, youth, their caregivers and communities, as well as reduce systemic barriers for population to mental health in Canada. The program uses a multi-phased approach to fund the testing and delivery of innovative, community-based interventions that aim to promote health equity, build protective factors and address the underlying determinants of health at the population level. Knowledge gained from the evaluation of each community-based intervention is then applied to public health policy and practice to accelerate change for positive mental health in Canada. The MHPIF also funds a Knowledge Development and Exchange (KDE) Hub that supports funded projects in sharing lessons learned, connecting with various stakeholders and applying new knowledge to a broader policy and system change agenda.
The Mental Health Promotion Innovation Fund funded the testing of 20 population health interventions in Phase 1, reaching over 50,000 individuals across the country. In 2022–23, 15 of these projects moved to Phase 2 to fully evaluate and implement their intervention. In 2023–24, these projects reached over 105,000 children, youth, caregivers, and families from over 200 communities across Canada. Early results from Phase 2 demonstrate that projects are impacting the protective factors to promote mental health including increased social-emotional development in children, increased coping, improved school engagement and improved mental health literacy. In addition, projects have moved beyond individual change to impact broader mental health promotion policies and practices, resulting in sustainable change for the communities in which they work.
Performance Indicator Results (based on most recent data collected in 2023):
- In Phase 2 of the MHPIF, 15 population health interventions were developed and/or adapted;
- 93% of projects engaged in multisectoral partnerships including provincial departments of health, universities, school boards, health authorities and units, child development centres, Indigenous health centres and local food establishments;
- 53% of projects leveraged funds from other sources, totaling over $11 million from other funding sources;
- 93% of projects reported increased knowledge and/or skills among participants and 70% of participants surveyed reported an increase in knowledge and/or skills;
- 60% of projects reported increased protective factors among participants and 43% of participants surveyed reported an increase in protective factors;
- 53% of projects reported improved well-being among participants and 52% of participants surveyed reported improved well-being;
- 33% of projects have sites in more than 3 provinces and/or territories;
- 98% of stakeholders surveyed reported using the knowledge to inform their practices or policies; and
- In the first year of Phase 2, 73% of projects provided examples on how the evidence or population health intervention research findings generated by their project influenced policy, practice and/or programs.

#### Métis Nation Health Data
Type of transfer payment: Grants
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates

- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
This program will support the Métis Nation in building capacity for sustainable health data monitoring and reporting systems within their governments by ensuring that the necessary partnerships and resources are in place to gather and analyze health data related to their citizens. Métis Nation-specific health data will provide evidence to further support policies and programs for health service delivery that is culturally responsive with the ultimate goal of improving health outcomes for the Métis Nation.
Six grant agreements were signed with the Métis National Council, the Métis Nations of Ontario, Saskatchewan, Alberta, British Columbia and the Manitoba Métis Federation. Each organization has been able to hire new human resources; use data to inform their policies, programs and proposals; formalize agreements to gather and analyze health data; and share knowledge through conferences, forums and meetings.
The Métis National Council (MNC)
The MNC focussed on establishing a strong foundation for data governance and promoting culturally responsive health care policies, programs, and services to improve Métis health outcomes. They collaborated with industry to create a data strategy framework to better handle Métis Nation citizen data. Additionally, the MNC is developing the Métis Data Principles report which describes an inventory of data sovereignty practices by each Governing Members within the Métis Nation, an environmental scan of Indigenous data sovereignty nationally and internationally as well as National Data Principles. Finally, the MNC has established important partnerships on health data with the Canadian Institutes of Health Research as well as the Canadian Institute for Health Information.
- The Métis Nation of Ontario (MNO) cemented collaborative partnerships to support health data with Cancer Care Ontario, the Ontario Renal Network, and ICES. They completed the first study " Monitoring Cardiovascular Disease in Métis Citizens Across Ontario, 2012-2020 " that investigated the trends in cardiovascular outcomes among MNO citizens in a decade.
- The Manitoba Métis Federation (MMF) used preliminary results from their Regional Health Survey to inform negotiations regarding legislation and guide the delivery of specific MMF program proposals and service creation in areas such as mental health programming, diabetes testing and program development, and sports-based programming.
- The Métis Nation of Saskatchewan (MNS) developed a wide range of publicly available knowledge products detailing Métis experiences with the provincial health care system, linkages between Métis culture and identity and health promotion, promoting oral wellness and harm reduction.
- In Alberta, the Métis Nation of Alberta (MNA) leveraged their project work to secure infrastructure funding that is grounded in the needs uncovered by their own health data. The Memorandums of Understanding and/or of Relational Understandings that have been created with the University of Alberta's various faculties have highlighted that the MNA is viewed as a leader, and the MNA have been invited to support the development of a new Bachelor of health science degree that will have a Metis-specific component.
- Finally in British Columbia (BC), the Métis Nation of British Columbia (MNBC) continued to advance the Métis Public Health Program by signing a Letter of Understanding with the BC Office of the Provincial Health Officer in order to track physical, mental, emotional, social, and spiritual health outcomes for Métis people living in BC. MNBC has presented on the unique cultural safety considerations when working with Métis patients in healthcare settings to the Doctors of BC.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this project is to address significant knowledge gaps in mpox antiviral treatment through rapid response research, in collaboration with local public health authorities and priority populations disproportionately impacted by the outbreak. Improving understanding of mpox, treatment and the outbreak in Canada is essential to inform national and global response. This research is recommended by the WHO through a Research and Development Blueprint on Mpox and an expert panel on mpox established by PHAC's Chief Public Health Officer and Chief Science Officer.
This contribution program supported rapid research to address gaps in clinical and epidemiological knowledge of the mpox virus and antiviral treatments, through a study conducted at the Research Institute of the McGill University Health Centre (RIMUHC). The study proposal included a randomized control trial for antiviral treatment and a prospective observational cohort study to monitor various impacts of mpox infection.
In 2023–2024, the RIMUHC monitored the public health landscape and ensured readiness of their sites for recruitment as new cases emerge. In January 2024, an increase in the provincial incidence of mpox was observed in Ontario, which began active enrollment of participants in the study.

#### National Microbiology Laboratory – Integral Genomics Innovation Program
Type of transfer payment: Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2021–22
Link to departmental result:
- Result 2.2: Infectious disease outbreaks and threats are prepared for and responded to effectively
Link to the department's program inventory:
- Laboratory Science Leadership and Services
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this program is to strengthen public health infrastructure in Canada by helping public health laboratories to innovate and integrate genome-sequencing productivity across surveillance of high-consequence pathogens. The program complements existing partnerships by accelerating the speed of genomic sequencing and integrating its outputs within existing public health surveillance activities.
This funding opportunity aims to mobilize public health networks to enhance the generation and availability of genomic information for the detection and tracking of evolution within high-consequence pathogens (e.g., COVID-19, tuberculosis and antimicrobial resistance). The Integral Genomics Innovation Program will improve the completeness and timely availability of genomic information on emerging variants and supports PHAC's work with provinces, territories and international partners to improve monitoring, public health surveillance and outbreak response across Canada.
This funding opportunity will support the following program objectives:
- Accelerate the availability of analysis-ready genomics (sequences plus contextual data) within a province or territory;
- Improve the completeness, quality and maintainability of contextual data supporting genomics to better enable use of genomics in effective public health responses;
- Improve the bidirectional relationship between laboratory-generated sequences and health authority data to enable genomic epidemiology analyses (e.g., vaccine effectiveness by variant);
- Facilitate the timely dissemination, exchange and application of analysis-ready information with public health partners; and
- Contribute to a coherent and effective genomic surveillance network across Canada.
Investment in provincial and territorial projects through the Integral Genomics Innovation Program improved turnaround time, increased productivity through more cost-effective operations and enabled stronger collaboration between the National Microbiology Laboratory (NML) at PHAC and provincial laboratories. During 2023–24, the number of laboratories supported by PHAC that engaged in genomic innovation increased from five to eight, as three additional laboratories took on the challenge of adapting to the new and different capabilities accessible through operational whole genome sequencing.
During the first year of investment, the median turnaround time from sample collection to availability of analysis-ready SARS-CoV-2 (the virus that causes COVID-19) sequence data was brought below 30 days but had significant variability over 60 days. Through that first year, median turnaround time stabilized at 19 days with a variability of 15 days. In fiscal year 2022–23, the median turnaround time was reduced to 16 days, with less than six days of variability. Moreover, these improved turnaround times remained consistent into 2023–24 while work to incorporate other pathogens was also undertaken, including the sequencing of influenza, respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), tuberculosis and streptococcus-A in different laboratories based on local priorities. In addition, data management and reporting tools necessary for local provincial purposes, public reporting and data sharing with NML completed key milestones and, for some provinces, became operational in 2023–24, ahead of the final year of the current phase of the program.
Several of the provincial laboratories being supported by the Integral Genomics Innovation Program are investing in linkage of laboratory sequence data and epidemiological case data, with significant progress being made by many, particularly the British Columbia Centre for Disease Control (BCCDC) and Institut national de santé publique du Québec (INSPQ). These laboratories have demonstrated linkage infection transmission patterns (ancestral trees) with geography, age, vaccination status and severe outcomes, which were shared with other Integral partners at a Genomic Innovation Summit held at NML in November 2023. This progress is foundational for robust linkage in all provinces and territories, which is a necessary pre-cursor to building national linked-surveillance capabilities that allow emerging changes in pathogen susceptibility and outcomes to be more rapidly and precisely detected. Furthermore, Integral Genomics Innovation investments in the data generation, collection, curation, quality control and reporting contributed to the completeness and accuracy of the contextual data (i.e., parameters such as sequencing date and purpose of sequencing) used for genomic data analysis. Prior to the Integral investments, only 73% of contextual data were available. This improved to 82% in fiscal year 2022–23 and is approaching 100% in fiscal year 2023–24.
The Integral Genomics Innovation Program is also investing in the standardisation of contextual data transferred from provinces to the NML to further improve the utility of analysis-ready genomics. The program supports the diversification and further adoption of the Data Harmonizer ontology and associated tools across public health genomics in Canada and internationally. Used as part of the COVID-19 sequence sharing environment, this tool was adapted to address sequence sharing of mpox in fiscal year 2022–23. In 2023–24, the tool was adopted by several international partners and the recipients of the contribution support are also in discussion to establish international standards based on the work supported.
The combination of data harmonizer progress with provincial performance improvements for contextual data are essential for meaningful analysis of pathogen genomics, curation and cleaning efforts. This had previously been outsourced since 2021 to a third party but was brought in-house to NML teams during 2023–24. By leveraging the progress made by individual provincial laboratories with the acceleration of data harmonisation leadership via contribution by Simon Fraser University, the processes and tools prototyped during the COVID-19 pandemic have been stabilised and generalised. During the final year of this phase of the Integral program, it is expected that operational efficiencies from consistent data flows via standardised contextual data tools will extend to other respiratory pathogens and wastewater, benefiting both the provincial recipients of contribution funding and nationally consistent information flows.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
This program supports culturally appropriate retail and community-based nutrition education initiatives to influence healthy eating in isolated northern communities, as part of the Nutrition North Canada (NNC) program led by Crown-Indigenous Relations and Northern Affairs Canada (CIRNAC).
The objective of the program is to increase knowledge of healthy eating, support skills in the selection and preparation of healthy store-bought and traditional or country food and build on existing community-based activities.
PHAC continues to support NNC by providing funding for culturally appropriate community-based nutrition education initiatives to 10 isolated northern communities that are outside of the mandate of Indigenous Services Canada's (ISC) First Nations and Inuit Health Branch. In 2023–24, funding recipients delivered a total of 1,170 nutrition education activities to more than 3,400 participants. These activities included cooking programs focused on food skills development, gardening and local harvesting activities, healthy offerings and promoting nutrition, and healthy food information via social media, and community engagement through nutrition information sharing. Results include:
- Funding recipients involved multiple population groups in various activities across PHAC's 10 communities, with approximately 16% involving children, 9.5% involving youth, 49% involving adults and 25.5% involving older adults. Participation increased in 2023–24 from previous years as recipients have recovered from the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on community activities; and
- Progress continues to be made towards strengthening the nutritional choices and overall health of these 10 communities as 90% of funding recipients reported that community members gained knowledge and skills related to healthy eating, because of these nutrition education activities during this year.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose is to develop evidence on complex public health issues that will support decision making across Canada.
The objective is to support the development of state of evidence on complex pan-Canadian public health issues such as Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) and health data sharing.
This program supported two expert panel reports conducted by the Council of Canadian Academies (CCA). Overcoming Resistance: Expert Panel on Antimicrobial Availability was released in September 2023 and described the challenges Canada faced when accessing high-value antimicrobials and the pull incentives that could help boost the availability of novel antimicrobials going forward. Connecting the Dots: The Expert Panel on Health Data Sharing was released in October 2023 and explored how Canada can modernize its current approach to health data sharing while continuing to protect the privacy of personal health information.
Both studies were the result of several expert panel meetings, peer review with comprehensive responses, development of the consensus reports that met CCA standards and extensive knowledge mobilization.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program
The Contribution Agreement with McMaster University was established on January 27, 2023. The evidence-based guidelines for post COVID-19 condition (PCC) aim to develop, disseminate and evaluate six comprehensive, evidence-based guidelines on topics covering the full cycle of the condition. The objectives of the Post COVID-19 Condition (CAN-PCC) program include:
- Developing six evidence-based guidelines on PCC;
- Raising awareness about these guidelines and promote their uptake; and
- Ensuring effectiveness of these guidelines on the ultimate beneficiaries.
Guideline Team Development and Public Engagement Activities
- Finalized the six guideline topics (Prevention of PCC; Testing, identification and diagnosis related to PCC; Clinical interventions for PCC; Neurological and psychiatric topics; Pediatric and adolescent topics; Health care services and systems, social support), along with selected chairs, co-chairs, methodological experts, and panel members for each topic team while ensuring patient representation.
- Conducted public and stakeholder consultation to gather feedback on topic prioritization, research questions, Good Practice Statements (GPS) and draft recommendations before final publication.
Engagement with PCC Research Landscape
- Engaged with other entities in the PCC research landscape, such as Long COVID Web leaders and the World Health Organization, through the creation of an International Advisory Board (IAB), Equity Oversight Committee (EOC), and Guideline Development Group (GDG) to help with reviewing the recommendations.
Engagement with CCMOH and FPT Partners
- Engaged the Canadian Council of Medical Officers of Health (CCMOH) and federal, provincial and territorial partners, including the Health Support Committee and the Conference of Deputy Ministers of Health, to integrate feedback throughout the project lifecycle.
Good Practice Statements/Recommendations:
- Eleven Good Practice Statements were published.
- Conducted public consultation to gather feedback on three draft recommendations.
Knowledge Mobilization Tools
- Created a public-facing CAN-PCC website .
- Released videos explaining CAN-PCC methods and good practice statements on the website.

#### Promoting Health Equity: Mental Health of Black Canadians
Type of transfer payment: Grants and Contributions
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates

Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Promoting Health Equity: Mental Health of Black Canadians Fund (MHBC) supports Black people in Canada to develop more culturally focused knowledge, capacity and programs to improve mental health and its determinants for Black communities in Canada. The objectives of the fund are to:
- Increase understanding of the unique barriers to and social determinants of mental health for Black people in Canada;
- Increase knowledge of effective, culturally focused approaches and programs for improving mental health and addressing its key social determinants for Black people in Canada, including a focus on youth and their families and community environments; and
- Increase capacity within Black Canadian communities to address barriers to mental health.
MHBC supported community-based organizations, researchers and Black communities to generate new evidence on culturally focused programs and interventions that address mental health and its determinants for Black people in Canada.
In 2023–24, PHAC's MHBC continued to invest in 13 projects in communities across Canada. Examples of funded projects which ended in 2023–24 include:
- The University of Ottawa provided accredited training in anti-racist care and culturally adapted practices to mental health professionals, reaching a total of over 10,500 professionals since 2020. The University of Ottawa also offered the ''Becoming Anti-Racist'' training for the general public. Over 100,000 people were trained during the project's period. This training was also implemented across 12 different school boards across Canada. The University of Ottawa published five academic journal articles regarding anti-racist training, prevalence and determinants of depression and anxiety.
- The MHBC Knowledge Mobilization Network, led by TAIBU Community Health Centre, launched the Black Wellness Network website, in February 2024. This website centralizes access to a wealth of knowledge regarding Black mental health including MHBC funded projects' resources.
- In collaboration with the TAIBU Community Health Centre and funded project leads, PHAC published a series of Project Stories and infographics that summarize some of the funded projects and results from the MHBC Program. Preliminary results from the MHBC Program indicated that 61% of participants, the majority of which were women, reported improvement of their mental health as well as 71% of participants reported increasing their knowledge of mental health.
- The MHBC Knowledge Mobilization Network held their second annual conference, ACT Now, in March 2024, which brought together over 500 participants including Black leaders, Black elders, Black youth, academics, and mental health professionals from across Canada. Participants shared knowledge, expertise, learned, and advocated for Black mental health and wellness.
- The Aspire for Higher (A4H) Youth Mental Wellness program, provided a safe space for youth to identify, develop, and strengthen coping mechanisms, thereby supporting youth mental and physical health. The program implemented a physical activity and mental wellness curriculum, which used an anti-Black racism lens. 86% of youth participants reported on the post program survey that they were more knowledgeable about mental wellness after they participated in the program. 84% of youth participants also reported that their mental health had improved as a result of participating in the program.

#### Strengthening the Baby-Friendly Initiative
Type of transfer payment: Grants
This grant was funded as one of four priorities of the President and Chief Public Health Officer that could be advanced through short-term investments using surplus funds in the Agency.
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018–19
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this grant is to promote maternal and infant health in Canada by strengthening Canada's implementation of the Baby-Friendly Initiative, an evidence-based global initiative that has proven to be effective for increasing breastfeeding rates.
The program objectives are to:
- Modernize the Baby-Friendly Initiative in Canada to reflect updated guidance from the World Health Organization and input from Canadian stakeholders;
- Adapt, test and evaluate a national quality improvement initiative to develop a model to support facilities in achieving the "Baby-Friendly" designation; and
- Raise awareness of the Baby-Friendly Initiative in Canada.
An evaluation of the National Baby-Friendly Initiative Quality Improvement Collaborative Project was completed in 2022. The aim of the project was to adapt, implement and test a quality improvement framework, by building an integrated network of facilities that supported each other as they pursued Baby-Friendly designation. The evaluation found significant improvements consistently across Baby-Friendly Initiative (BFI) indicators. This included:
- 118% improvement in the proportion of families receiving assistance and support for breastfeeding within six hours of birth;
- 90.4% improvement in rooming in/ mother babies staying together;
- 82.8% improvement in skin to skin within five minutes of caesarean birth;
- 73.3% improvement in skin to skin for at least an hour after caesarean births; and
- 46.1% improvement in practicing skin to skin for at least an hour after vaginal births.
Improvement in critical success factors that supported the ability to advance BFI goals were also found. These included improvements in teamwork, commitment of leadership, the ability to collect and analyze data, parent partner voices, sharing and learning with other facilities across the country and support from the Breastfeeding Committee for Canada (BCC). The achievements occurred against a backdrop of a global pandemic that diverted resources in all facilities to support the COVID-19 pandemic response. The evaluation results have been presented internationally.
In April 2022, a Coach Mentor Program was launched to build on and scale up the impacts achieve through the project. Through this program, participants in the initial BFI project are trained to support a new cohort of hospitals and community health services as they begin their process towards becoming BFI designated.
In 2023–24, additional funding was provided to increase the scope and impact of the work undertaken through the grant. This included supporting two national workshops, one aimed to build capacity for scaling up modernized tools for implementation of the BFI, while the other workshop engaged Indigenous health leaders to inform culturally safe strategies to scale up BFI and improve breastfeeding rates in Indigenous communities.
Efforts also continued to implement a communications strategy to raise awareness of the Baby-Friendly Initiative and build understanding of evidence based best-practices to promote, protect and support breastfeeding.

The Vaccine Community Innovation Challenge (VCIC) is a funding program to support creative and innovative projects from community voices to promote vaccine confidence and adherence to COVID-19 public health measures. VCIC successfully complemented and built upon efforts to strengthen the confidence of people living in Canada in vaccination against COVID-19 through public engagement activities that were community-based and culturally sensitive.

## Sex- and gender-based analysis plus
Sex- and Gender-Based Analysis Plus (SGBA Plus) – used interchangeably with the Government of Canada's Gender-Based Analysis Plus (GBA Plus) – is an analytical, intersectional process used by the Government of Canada's Health Portfolio to assess how determinants of health interact and intersect with each other and broader systems of power and oppression.
In 2018, Parliament passed the Canadian Gender Budgeting Act . The departmental plans and departmental results reports are being used to fulfill the President of the Treasury Board's obligations to make public, every year, analysis on the impacts of expenditure programs on gender and diversity.
Each organization is responsible for conducting their own Gender-Based Analysis Plus (GBA Plus).
The Policy on Results indicates that Program officials, as designated by Deputy Heads, are responsible for ensuring data collection for meeting policy requirements.
All Government of Canada organizations must complete (S)GBA Plus supplementary information tables in departmental plans and departmental results reports on an annual basis.

### Section 1: Institutional SGBA Plus governance and capacity
PHAC is committed to applying an intersectional SGBA Plus approach to systematically integrate health equity in all policies, programs and initiatives.
In 2023–24, PHAC's SGBA Plus Focal Point, alongside program areas, advanced the 2022–25 PHAC SGBA Plus Action Plan Framework and Implementation Strategy in the following four priority areas:
- Enhanced SGBA Plus accountability in planning, reporting and decision-making processes;
- Promoted and enabled the collection and use of disaggregated data and scientific evidence for intersectional analyses;
- Strengthened awareness, understanding and capacity for SGBA Plus through tailored training and tools; and
- Developed strategic partnerships and collaborations to enhance equity-informed policy and culturally relevant policies and programming.
As per the 2022–25 PHAC SGBA Plus Action Plan Framework and Implementation Strategy, PHAC oversaw the establishment of a new executive-led governance table, comprised of executive-level SGBA Plus Branch Leads who led the development of SGBA Plus Branch Integration Plans (BIPs).
The PHAC SGBA Plus Champion, alongside the Executive executive-level SGBA Plus Branch Leads, continued to support the systematic integration of SGBA Plus into the organization's functions and programs, affirming the Agency's commitment to health equity. PHAC continued to integrate Equity, Diversity and Inclusion (EDI) considerations in programs and operations' decision-making processes by promoting SGBA Plus discussions at various governance committees and demonstrating progress on SGBA Plus commitments through an annual report to the Agency's Executive Committee.
PHAC continued to ensure the integration of SGBA Plus and EDI considerations and the use of inclusive, non-stigmatizing language, in the development of Memoranda to Cabinet, Budget Proposals and Treasury Board Submissions. Accountability mechanisms, including an internal SGBA Plus attestation process, increased both the quality and accuracy of the SGBA Plus analyses for Cabinet documents, while emphasizing the value of incorporating EDI considerations throughout PHAC policies and programs.
PHAC advanced its work to support the implementation of the renewed Health Portfolio Sex- and Gender-Based Plus Policy . For example, PHAC further embedded EDI into research design; prioritized the data collection and analysis of disaggregated data to inform policy and program design; and engaged with diverse stakeholders and partners to better understand lived and living experiences in PHAC's programs and initiatives. The SGBA Plus Focal Point continued to work with program areas to enable a more fulsome culture shift that will ensure a more systematic integration of EDI considerations into all aspects of the Agency's work.
PHAC continued to advance its SGBA Plus plans and priorities with a primary focus on strengthening capacity building and integrating SGBA Plus in various elements of program delivery.
Followed by the transformative refresh and renewal of the Health Portfolio SGBA Plus Policy, PHAC continued to implement the commitments outlined in the 2022–25 SGBA Plus Action Plan Framework and Implementation, including:
- Producing SGBA Plus evidence scans on PHAC priority areas (e.g. climate change and substance use);
- Supporting the development of the BIPs;
- Developing and disseminating a SGBA Plus Learning Path and performance objectives and indicators for Executives and employees;
- Hosting dialogue sessions to identify opportunities to strengthen SGBA Plus integration in disaggregated data collection, analysis, interpretation and reporting; and
- Developing and delivering tailored SGBA Plus training, presentations, resources and tools.
In 2023–24, the Agency's SGBA Plus Focal Point delivered 57 SGBA Plus presentations and training sessions to various audiences, reaching over 1,400 employees from regional offices, communities of practice and various senior management committees. The SGBA Plus Focal Point also completed a SGBA Plus review of over 45 Cabinet documents and public health guidance documents. To further support the application and capacity-building of SGBA Plus across the Agency, new tools were launched, including a guide of key considerations for applying intersectionality theory to partner and stakeholder engagement, a guide for applicants on integrating health equity into funding proposals and an internal SGBA Plus Learning Path, providing PHAC employees with a framework to inform the development of their SGBA Plus competencies within the Agency.
To measure progress on the Agency's implementation of SGBA Plus, the SGBA Plus Focal Point coordinated the Agency's submission for the 2022–23 Women and Gender Equality Canada (WAGE) GBA Plus Implementation Survey. Results from the submission highlighted exemplary practices of GBA Plus application and opportunities to strengthen GBA Plus capacity and implementation across PHAC.
The PHAC SGBA Plus Network, with over 85 representatives from across PHAC's Centres/Directorates, continued to advance and improve the systemic integration of SGBA Plus across PHAC through strengthened expertise from formal and applied learnings. This work was further supported by the PHAC SGBA Plus Planning Committee, with over 20 executive-level representatives and coordinators from each of PHAC's Branches who led the development of the BIPs.

#### Core Responsibility 1: Health promotion and chronic disease prevention
Program name: Chronic Disease Prevention
Program goals: This Program seeks new solutions for the complex challenge of preventing chronic diseases in Canada. The program works across sectors to leverage partnerships and investments to design, implement and scale interventions that support healthy behaviours and create social and physical environments that enable and promote better health. The program addresses behavioural risk factors for chronic disease, namely physical inactivity, unhealthy eating and tobacco use, among people who face health inequalities and are at greater risk of developing poor health outcomes, including the main chronic diseases (e.g., diabetes, cardiovascular disease, cancer, etc.). The program also aims to improve the mental health of Black people in Canada and support collective action on the social determinants of health to reduce health inequities. Ultimately, the Program contributes to improving health equity and preventing chronic diseases through action on the social determinants of health and effective healthy living policies and interventions to advance health outcomes for all.
Target population: All people in Canada that face health inequalities and inequities and are at greater risk of chronic disease.

Other key program impacts:
Postal code data collected from projects (reflected project activities implemented in 2022–23) were linked to several social-ecological indicators from the 2016 Canadian Census using the Canadian Alliance for Social Connection and Health database:

SGBA Plus data collection plan:
Healthy Canadians and Communities Fund (HCCF)
Organizations funded through the HCCF must report the number of individuals reached by gender (female, male, or other gender), age groups (children and youth, adults, older adults) and geographical location (the first three digits of postal codes).
Collecting additional sociodemographic factors to support equity-informed analyses such as employment status, education level, income/income range, immigration status and sexual orientation, are optional. However, projects are encouraged to collect this information if appropriate and relevant to support a more comprehensive equity-informed analysis and understanding of health and social inequities affecting project participants. To bridge data gaps, the HCCF links postal code data collected from projects to several socioeconomic indicators from the 2016 Canadian Census using the Canadian Alliance for Social Connection and Health database.
Funded projects are also encouraged to collect disaggregated data on any outcomes related to knowledge, skills, social environments, health behaviours and health status. Disaggregated outcomes are reported upon availability of data and on a voluntary basis as this was not a program requirement when most funded organizations started their projects. Since 2021–22, newly funded organizations must report this information in a more systemic manner and this reporting requirement is now embedded in contribution agreements.
To strengthen SGBA Plus integration, the HCCF contracted a consultant in 2022–23 to conduct an environmental scan and key informant interviews with funding recipients to identify barriers and best practices in collecting data disaggregated by sociodemographic factors to support an equity-informed analysis. In 2023–24, the consultant presented their findings, including resources and best practices for newly funded recipients. The report also included considerations for the HCCF regarding clear communication of data collection requirements, more flexible reporting templates and better integration of qualitative data in program performance.
Promoting Health Equity: Mental Health of Black Canadians Fund
The Mental Health of Black Canadians (MHBC) Fund applied SGBA Plus and integrated an equity-informed analysis to all elements of program design, implementation and evaluation. All funded projects were required to describe how their project design, recruitment and implementation considers health equity and includes equity-informed reporting as part of their project results.
Projects considered wide ranging needs and perspectives of the diverse Black communities they worked with and were able to adjust and tailor project activities accordingly. PHAC used this information to reflect on the design and delivery of the program, lessons learned regarding approaches to addressing underlying social determinants of health and potential implications for program delivery in other contexts.
Results from the MHBC projects will help to inform program and policy decisions by documenting the success of interventions, their context and the populations they reached. This information will also help service providers and others to adapt activities to best meet the needs of diverse Black communities across Canada. All projects funded through this program ended on March 31, 2024.
An example of SGBA Plus in action is the Trauma informed care to promote mental health equity for African, Caribbean and Black (ACB) 2SLGBTQIA+ community project:
- Led by the Black Coalition for AIDS Prevention and aims to address the mental health counselling needs of ACB 2SLGBTQIA+ community members who are experiencing mental health issues;
- Reached a diverse population of adult males, females, gender nonconforming/non-binary, 2SLGBTQIA+, Indigenous and ACB people;
- Provided culturally appropriate, trauma-informed screening and support that promotes retention in care by encouraging engagement. Intersectoral Action Fund (ISAF)
Equity is a key guiding principle in the ISAF program design. This includes the requirement for all funding recipients to integrate equity into the design and delivery of their funded projects. In 2023–24, ISAF launched a targeted solicitation where program officials provided additional supportive resources to applicants, including an Applicant Resource Guide and an Invitation to Submit a Funding Request checklist, to support capacity in terms of understanding key health equity principles, and how these principles could be integrated throughout project design and delivery.
Program name: Evidence for Health Promotion, and Chronic Disease and Injury Prevention – Canadian Post COVID-19 Condition (CAN-PCC) Guidelines
Program goals: The evidence-based guidelines for post COVID-19 condition (PCC) aim to develop, disseminate and evaluate six comprehensive, evidence-based guidelines on topics covering the full cycle of the condition. Further program objectives include raising awareness about guidelines and promote their uptake as well as ensuring effectiveness of these guidelines on the ultimate beneficiaries.
Target population: The target population for the CAN-PCC guidelines includes all people living in Canada, with a focus on priority populations such as Indigenous Peoples, refugees and migrants, 2SLGBTQI+ individuals, older adults in need of care, women, pregnant women and people, racialized groups, justice-involved people, people with no fixed housing, and those living in rural and remote communities.
Distribution of benefits:
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Third group: no significant distributional impacts
Second group: no significant intergenerational impacts or impacts on generations between youths and seniors
Specific demographic group outcomes:
When evidence is available, the CAN-PCC collaborative will develop recommendations for equity-deserving groups such as Indigenous Peoples, refugees, migrants, and 2SLGBTQIA+ individuals, addressing their unique healthcare needs and barriers for each guideline topic. Different age groups are also considered when developing the recommendations, including separate ones for children.
Key program impacts on gender and diversity:
The CAN-PCC guidelines integrate equity considerations throughout the development process, ensuring that gender and diversity impacts are central to the recommendations. This involves prioritizing evidence and relevant information related to equity-deserving groups to inform the drafting of equity considerations for the guidelines of the program. Additionally, an Equity Oversight Committee, comprising representatives of priority populations and community leaders, has been established to support the interpretation of evidence for recommendation development.
Other key program impacts:
The recommendations are developed and reviewed with input from patients panel members with lived and living experiences, ensuring that considerations around access, feasibility and acceptability of the interventions are addressed. This ensures that health equity is integral to the recommendation development and implementation process.
SGBA Plus data collection plan:
As part of the solicitation process, projects were required to integrate SGBA Plus considerations into their proposals, which were assessed during the screening process, and were encouraged to collect and use available disaggregated data on relevant variables. Recipients also provide internal progress reports related to SGBA Plus aspects, such as sex, gender, age, Indigenous status, disability, urban vs. rural status, level of education, employment status, general health status, primary language spoken and ethnicity. Summaries of projects are posted online by PHAC.
Program name: Evidence for Health Promotion, and Chronic Disease and Injury Prevention – Integrated Strategy for Healthy Living and Chronic Disease – Enhanced Surveillance for Chronic Disease
Program goals: The Enhanced Surveillance for Chronic Disease Program aims is to build surveillance capacity and fill data gaps in priority areas. Under the terms of contribution agreements established, recipients will be responsible for submitting progress reports semi-annually, until they close.
Target population: Key populations including 2SLGBTQIA+, people living in situations of vulnerability, ethnic minorities, older adults (i.e., 65 and over), rural and remote populations, populations living with low socio-economic status and other populations where data are limited.
Distribution of benefits:
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Third group: no significant distributional impacts
Second group: no significant intergenerational impacts or impacts on generations between youths and seniors
Specific demographic group outcomes:
Persistent gaps remain in chronic disease and/or risk factor surveillance related to demographic groups, which are not effectively captured through current surveys or traditional methodologies.
The program has placed a focus on key populations in its solicitations, including Indigenous populations, institutionalized populations, 2SLGBTQIA+ communities, rural and remote populations, racialized groups, recent immigrants/new people in Canada and other populations where evidence is lacking for specific topics (e.g., children, youth, older adults, etc.).
Key program impacts on gender and diversity:
The Homelessness Counts study aims to access and integrate existing sources of information to better determine who, and how many people in Canada are experiencing homelessness. Given the variations among people experiencing homelessness across the country, especially within rural and remote regions, the study is striving to obtain a Canada wide sample to better understand people experiencing homelessness and their needs. The Lawson Health Research Institute interviewed 400 people experiencing homelessness and 200 community stakeholders and service providers in 28 distinct communities across Canada. A website has been developed to serve as a platform to share research findings and publications as they become available.
The Community-Based Research Centre (CBRC) has recruited participants for Our Health , a national online quantitative survey study, by leveraging the return of in-person 2SLGBTQIA+ Pride events since the beginning of the pandemic. Staff, volunteers and representatives from partner organizations connected with community members at Pride events to share information about Our Health and draw attention to the need for additional data on how 2SLGBTQIA+ communities are impacted by chronic health conditions. Additionally, the CBRC have had a presence in a wide variety of media to talk about the need to collect data on chronic health conditions for 2SLGBTQIA+ communities, and the community-based approach CBRC has utilized. This includes The Tyee (which was then picked up the Toronto Star), the CanQueer podcast, HomoCulture, CTV, CBNnoticias (Spanish), The Meliorist, OMNI News (with a second show in Punjabi and a third show in Mandarin), Monsoon Journal, CBC News, CBC Radio, and Healthing.ca.
SGBA Plus data collection plan:
As part of the solicitation processes, funded organizations integrated SGBA Plus considerations into their projects. Some examples of information being captured for analysis in this program include sex, gender, age, Indigenous status, disability, urban vs. rural status, level of education, employment status, general health status, including diagnosed mental health conditions, and race and immigration status.
Program name: Health Promotion – Community Action Program for Children (CAPC) and Canada Prenatal Nutrition Program (CPNP)
Program goals: The CAPC and CPNP provide funding to community groups to support pregnant women and people, children aged 0 to 6 and their parents and guardians who experience challenges that place their health at risk.
Funded projects provide stigma-free, culturally safe and inclusive environments, applying health equity and social determinants of health-based approaches to encourage healthy pregnancies, positive parenting and healthy child development in populations facing health equity barriers.
Target population: Pregnant women and people, children (aged 0 to 6) and their families who face challenges that put their health at risk. These conditions may include low socioeconomic status, teenage pregnancy or parenthood, social or geographic isolation, recent arrival to Canada and situations of violence or neglect. Special emphasis is placed on the inclusion of Indigenous families living in urban and rural communities.
Distribution of benefits:
Fourth group: 60% to 79% women
First group: strongly benefits individuals living on low-income (strongly progressive)
First group: primarily benefits youth, children or future generations
Specific demographic group outcomes:
CAPC and CPNP directly benefit pregnant women and people, children aged 0 to 6 and their parents and guardians who experience health related risks. and face increased barriers in accessing supports and programs due to systemic inequalities. Funded projects aim to decrease barriers to participation by providing stigma-free, culturally safe and inclusive environments.

#### Core Responsibility 2: Infectious disease prevention and control
Program name: Communicable Disease and Infection Control
Program goals: The purpose of the program is to prevent and control the spread of infectious disease in communities and healthcare settings. The program is striving to achieve this goal through collaboration with domestic and international stakeholders to share knowledge, tools and resources to support public health front-line efforts, including tailored community-based interventions, to prevent, control and reduce community and healthcare-associated infections. It promotes and coordinates a pan-Canadian approach to disaggregated data collection to help identify key populations and locations to provide evidence-informed public health measures guidance, address barriers to healthcare services related to sexually transmitted blood borne disease (STBBI) and harm reduction and reduce stigma and discrimination toward diverse populations disproportionally affected by STBBI, including people living with HIV or hepatitis C.
Target population: Health care professionals, public, provincial and territorial governments and non-governmental organizations.

Led by PHAC, the Community Action Fund (CAF) and Harm Reduction Fund (HRF) support time-limited projects (three to five years) across Canada to address HIV, hepatitis C and other sexually transmitted infections, and help reduce HIV and hepatitis C among people who share injection and inhalation drug-use equipment. Projects funded under CAF and HRF were reviewed by a committee comprised of individuals from key populations, people living with HIV, researchers and front-line workers who were selected to ensure representation and balance based on region, gender, age, population, expertise and experience. Funded projects consider intersecting identities for key populations as well as social determinants of health and impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the health inequities experienced by these groups, allowing for more tailored approaches and greater impact. Analysis of gender and diversity data collected through the final reports submitted by funded organizations during the previous CAF and HRF cycle (2017–22) is being undertaken with focus on results that describe the impact of the funded projects on targeted groups.
Many northern, remote, isolated and predominantly Indigenous communities lack many of the ancillary services (e.g., confirmatory testing, reporting of diagnostic test results, etc.) needed to effectively participate in and implement large scale initiatives. In 2022–23, PHAC secured $8 million to launch a feasibility study to purchase and distribute HIV self-test (HIVST) kits through community-based organizations to increase the number of people in Canada who could access testing. By the end of March 2023, PHAC provided 70,535 HIVST kits to participating community-based organizations across Canada who distributed 10,343 kits to individuals and will continue to do so throughout 2023–24. The approach built on the capacity of community-based organizations to reach key populations who encounter significant barriers to accessing testing and treatment. The initiative has shown an increase in testing offers, including among those who are unaware of their disease status. It has also demonstrated the value of offering low-barrier access to testing to help identify the undiagnosed, with a reported 46% of respondents indicating that they had never been tested previously. In one survey of test users, 98% of participants reported being satisfied with the program while almost 50% of participants indicated that they had obtained additional kits to distribute among their social and sexual networks.
PHAC continues to engage with key representatives, partners and stakeholders throughout a project's process to ensure that surveillance data and findings are appropriately contextualized in a culturally relevant and safe manner.
Community-based organizations are particularly well positioned to reach key populations because of existing relationships with the populations they serve, allowing for a greater likelihood that people would have the necessary supports and linkages to care following the screening result. Information shared with program officers during consultative processes and findings from completed surveys included findings from the national surveys conducted by PHAC among African, Black and Caribbean (ACB) people and People who use drugs (PWUD) published in September 2022 and February 2023. These results accentuate the need to apply an equity lens and develop public health interventions that address barriers to STBBI-related and other services that are rooted within social determinants of health.

In general, laboratory services involve testing samples from clients who were not required to submit patient information and metrics, such as gender, for privacy reasons. Where possible, research and testing activities considered SGBA Plus on a project-by-project basis.
Positive impacts were achieved in 2023–24 in terms of providing equitable access to laboratory testing, particularly by creating additional testing capacity in remote and dispersed populations across Canada with limited access to testing facilities. NMLB regularly receives samples for diagnosis from the provinces and territories (PTs).
